class_schedule.topic: Strategic Human Resource Management and the HR Function
learning_objectives: Define strategic human resource management and explain how HR creates competitive advantage
Distinguish between transactional HR and strategic HR roles using the Ulrich model
Describe the major HRM functions: staffing, development, compensation, employee relations
Analyze how HR policies affect firm-level outcomes such as productivity, turnover, and engagement
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 1 and Ch. 3
Noe, Hollenbeck, Gerhart & Wright, Fundamentals of Human Resource Management (10th ed.), Ch. 1-2
Ulrich, Human Resource Champions (1996), Ch. 1-2
Huselid, 'The Impact of HR Management Practices on Turnover, Productivity, and Corporate Financial Performance,' Academy of Management Journal (1995)

lecture_notes.introduction: The question of whether HR is strategic has been answered definitively by research: firms with high-performance HR systems outperform competitors by wide margins on profitability, productivity, and market value. Mark Huselid's landmark 1995 study of nearly 1,000 firms found that a one standard deviation improvement in high-performance HR practices was associated with an 8% reduction in turnover and increased sales per employee of over $27,000. Google (Alphabet) built its competitive advantage partly on its People Operations function, which used data-driven hiring and management practices to assemble and retain one of the most productive workforces in business history. Costco's wage strategy, paying substantially above Walmart's Sam's Club, produces lower turnover, higher productivity, and better customer service, contributing to a gross margin that supports its low-price positioning. At the opposite extreme, firms that treat HR as a pure compliance and paperwork function miss enormous value: they hire slower, train worse, lose more employees, and face more lawsuits. This course examines HR as a strategic function that creates competitive advantage, not as an administrative cost to be minimized. You will learn the tools of HR work (job analysis, recruitment, selection, performance management, compensation, labor relations) and the analytical frameworks to evaluate whether those tools are producing the people capability your firm's strategy requires.
key_concepts: Strategic Human Resource Management (SHRM): the pattern of planned HR deployments and activities intended to enable an organization to achieve its goals; contrasts with reactive, transactional HR
HR as competitive advantage: when HR practices produce a workforce that is valuable, rare, inimitable, and well-organized (the VRIO test), they become a source of sustainable advantage

Ulrich's four HR roles: strategic partner (aligns HR with business strategy), administrative expert (efficient delivery of HR services), employee champion (advocates for employees), change agent (manages transformation)

High-performance work systems (HPWS): coherent bundles of HR practices (selective hiring, extensive training, performance-based pay, employee involvement) that produce superior outcomes through complementarity

The five major HRM functions: staffing (job analysis, recruitment, selection), human resource development (training, career management), compensation & benefits, employee & labor relations, safety & health
HR metrics and analytics: turnover rate, time-to-fill, cost per hire, revenue per employee, engagement scores, training ROI; the basis for evidence-based HR decisions

Line vs. staff HR: line managers make the actual people decisions (hire, fire, promote, coach); HR designs processes and provides expertise; tensions between the two are a constant management challenge
HR business partner model: HR generalists embedded in business units who translate strategy into HR implications and coach line managers, moving HR out of a purely central function
The employment relationship: balancing employer prerogative with employee rights and expectations; evolving under law, social norms, and economic change
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis of Google's People Operations. Google replaced brainteaser interviews with structured behavioral interviews after analytics showed brainteasers had zero predictive validity for job performance. Google's Project Oxygen identified the eight behaviors of effective managers through regression analysis of 10,000 performance reviews and tied each to training and coaching interventions. Compare to a failing firm (e.g., pre-turnaround Yahoo) where HR was transactional and hiring was manager-driven with no data discipline. Discuss what organizational conditions enable strategic HR and which block it.
setup_requirements: Sample HR policy documents for analysis

review.value: Decades of research link high-performance HR practices to bottom-line outcomes; firms that treat HR as strategic outperform those that do not, and the effect is not marginal but substantial
A single HR practice rarely moves the needle; coherent bundles that reinforce each other (selective hiring plus training plus performance pay plus engagement) create compounding effects
HR can design excellent processes, but line managers are the actual implementers; an HR strategy that does not engage line managers cannot succeed
HR practices that work in Silicon Valley may fail in a unionized manufacturing plant; strategic HR adapts practices to the firm's strategy, workforce, and environment

class_schedule.topic: Employment Law and Equal Employment Opportunity
learning_objectives: Identify the major federal employment laws and the protected classes they cover
Distinguish between disparate treatment and disparate impact discrimination
Apply the Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures and the four-fifths rule
Describe employer obligations under the ADA, FMLA, and ADEA
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 2
Noe et al., Fundamentals of HRM (10th ed.), Ch. 3
Civil Rights Act of 1964 (Title VII), ADA (1990), ADEA (1967), FMLA (1993) - primary texts
EEOC, 'Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures' (1978)

lecture_notes.introduction: Employment law defines the boundaries within which every HR decision must operate, and ignorance of those boundaries exposes firms to enormous liability. In 2023, the EEOC secured over $665 million in monetary benefits for discrimination victims; individual cases routinely settle for millions (Walmart's 2010 gender discrimination class action eventually cost the company hundreds of millions even after the Supreme Court denied class certification). Beyond monetary damages, employment law violations damage brand, reduce the talent pool willing to work for the firm, and signal a culture of negligence. The foundational framework in the U.S. is Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which prohibits employment discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin. Subsequent laws expanded protection to age (ADEA, 1967), disability (ADA, 1990), genetic information (GINA, 2008), and pregnancy. The Family and Medical Leave Act (1993) provides job protection for medical and family leave. Each law has specific triggers, covered employers, and enforcement mechanisms; HR professionals must know them cold. Beyond knowing the rules, HR must design selection, compensation, promotion, and termination processes that can withstand scrutiny: the question is not whether you will face an employment claim but when, and whether you have the documentation and defensible processes to prevail.
key_concepts: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964: prohibits discrimination by employers with 15+ employees based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin; enforced by the EEOC

Protected classes: race, color, religion, sex (including pregnancy, sexual orientation, and gender identity since Bostock v. Clayton County 2020), national origin, age (40+), disability, genetic information, veteran status
Disparate treatment: intentional discrimination against a member of a protected class; proven by direct evidence or by the McDonnell Douglas framework of circumstantial evidence

Disparate impact: facially neutral practice that has a disproportionately adverse effect on a protected class; does not require intent; triggered by the four-fifths rule (selection rate for any group below 80% of the highest group's rate)

Bona fide occupational qualification (BFOQ): narrow defense permitting otherwise discriminatory selection when the characteristic is essential to the job (e.g., sex for modeling female clothing); does not apply to race
ADA (Americans with Disabilities Act, 1990): prohibits discrimination against qualified individuals with disabilities; requires reasonable accommodation unless it imposes undue hardship

FMLA (Family and Medical Leave Act, 1993): 12 weeks of unpaid, job-protected leave for qualifying family and medical reasons; covers employers with 50+ employees; employee must have worked 1,250 hours in the past 12 months
ADEA (Age Discrimination in Employment Act, 1967): protects workers 40 and older from discrimination; applies to employers with 20+ employees
Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures (1978): technical standards for validating selection tests to defend against disparate impact claims
State and local laws: often go beyond federal law (e.g., California's FEHA, NYC Human Rights Law); employers must comply with the most protective applicable law
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply the four-fifths rule to a hiring test. Of 200 white applicants, 100 were hired (50% selection rate); of 100 Black applicants, 30 were hired (30% selection rate). 30/50 = 60%, below the 80% threshold, indicating disparate impact. The employer must now show the test is job-related and consistent with business necessity, or modify it. Compare to the Griggs v. Duke Power (1971) Supreme Court case that established disparate impact doctrine when Duke Power's high school diploma and test requirements excluded Black workers from higher-paying jobs without being job-related. Walk through what validation evidence (criterion-related, content, or construct validity) would defend the test.
setup_requirements: Case briefs: Griggs v. Duke Power, McDonnell Douglas v. Green, Bostock v. Clayton County
Sample job descriptions for BFOQ analysis

review.value: Disparate treatment requires intent; disparate impact does not. A well-meaning but poorly validated test can still be illegal if it disproportionately screens out a protected class without job-related justification

Employment law cases turn on documentation: job descriptions, interview notes, performance reviews, termination memos. HR's discipline in documentation determines whether a firm can defend its decisions

review.key: Reasonable Accommodation Is an Interactive Process
review.value: ADA requires employers to engage in a good-faith dialogue with employees about accommodation; failing to engage, not just failing to accommodate, creates liability

review.key: Federal is the Floor, Not the Ceiling
review.value: Many states and cities extend protection beyond federal law; multi-state employers must comply with the most protective applicable law in each jurisdiction

learning_objectives: Conduct a job analysis using multiple methods (observation, interview, questionnaires, O*NET)
Distinguish between job descriptions and job specifications
Apply job design principles including job enlargement, enrichment, and rotation
Use the Job Characteristics Model to analyze motivational potential of a job
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 4
Noe et al., Fundamentals of HRM (10th ed.), Ch. 4
Hackman & Oldham, Work Redesign (1980), Ch. 3-4
U.S. Department of Labor, O*NET Content Model documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Job analysis is the foundation of nearly every HR activity. Recruitment requires knowing what knowledge, skills, and abilities to look for. Selection requires knowing what the job actually entails so tests and interviews measure relevant attributes. Training requires knowing the skill gaps. Compensation requires knowing how jobs compare. Performance management requires knowing what behaviors and outcomes matter. Without a solid job analysis, all these activities proceed on guesswork, vulnerable to both bias and inefficiency. A poorly-analyzed job also produces mismatched hiring, wasted training, pay disputes, and legal exposure under the ADA (which requires identifying essential job functions). Beyond analyzing existing jobs, firms increasingly need to redesign jobs to match changing strategies and worker expectations. Toyota's assembly line revolution enriched narrowly-defined jobs into team-based roles where workers had authority to stop the line and solve problems. Zappos experimented with holacracy, eliminating traditional management roles entirely (though the experiment had mixed results). Amazon's warehouse jobs, engineered for throughput, have drawn criticism for their physical demands and lack of autonomy, illustrating the trade-off between operational efficiency and worker well-being. The Job Characteristics Model, developed by Hackman and Oldham, offers a rigorous framework for predicting how job design affects motivation, satisfaction, and productivity.
key_concepts: Job analysis: systematic process of determining the knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics required for a job, and the tasks, duties, and responsibilities the job comprises
Job description: written summary of the tasks, duties, and responsibilities of a job (what the jobholder does)
Job specification: written list of the knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics needed to perform the job (who can do it)

Job analysis methods: observation (watching the work), interviews (with incumbents and supervisors), questionnaires (PAQ, F-JAS), diaries/logs, critical incident technique, subject matter expert panels

O*NET (Occupational Information Network): U.S. Department of Labor database with detailed information on 900+ occupations; includes tasks, tools, knowledge, skills, abilities, work context, and education
Competency models: identify broader competencies (communication, leadership, problem-solving) needed across jobs in a family or organization, complementing traditional task-based analysis

Essential functions (ADA): job functions so fundamental that removing them would fundamentally change the job; legally relevant because ADA requires reasonable accommodation only for essential functions

Job design approaches: scientific management (Taylor, specialization and efficiency), job enlargement (more variety at same skill level), job enrichment (adding planning, control, and decision authority), job rotation (moving through multiple jobs)

Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Oldham): motivating potential depends on skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback; high MPS correlates with internal motivation, satisfaction, and performance
Motivating Potential Score (MPS) = [(Skill Variety + Task Identity + Task Significance)/3] * Autonomy * Feedback; zero on autonomy or feedback drives the whole score to zero
Telework and flexible work design: accelerated by COVID, now a permanent feature of many jobs; raises new questions about productivity, supervision, collaboration, and equity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Job analysis for a software developer role using multiple methods. Start with O*NET (occupation 15-1252.00 Software Developers) to pull tasks, tools, and knowledge areas. Interview two incumbents and one supervisor about their daily work, challenges, and what separates strong from weak performers. Identify critical incidents (times the job was done especially well or poorly). Synthesize into a job description with tasks and a job specification with KSAs. Then apply the Job Characteristics Model: rate the job on each dimension (skill variety: high; task identity: medium - developers often work on pieces of larger systems; task significance: varies; autonomy: moderate to high; feedback: good from code, weaker from users). Compute MPS and compare to a QA tester role to see how job design affects motivation.

review.value: Recruitment, selection, training, compensation, and performance management all assume you know what the job is; skipping job analysis guarantees errors downstream in every other HR activity
Job description answers 'what does this person do?'; job specification answers 'what kind of person can do it?' Both are needed and should be connected through explicit task-KSA linkages
Hackman and Oldham proved that job design has large effects on motivation and performance independent of pay; enriching jobs is often cheaper and more effective than raising wages
Under the ADA, employers must identify essential job functions in writing; failure to do so undermines the defense when an accommodation dispute arises

learning_objectives: Develop recruitment strategy aligned with workforce planning and business needs
Compare internal and external recruitment sources and their trade-offs
Evaluate recruitment sources using yield ratios, cost per hire, and quality of hire metrics
Design an employer brand and employee value proposition
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 5
Noe et al., Fundamentals of HRM (10th ed.), Ch. 5
Breaugh, Employee Recruitment: Current Knowledge and Important Areas for Future Research (2008)
Cable & Turban, 'The Value of Organizational Reputation in the Recruitment Context,' Journal of Applied Psychology (2003)

lecture_notes.introduction: Recruitment is where workforce strategy meets reality. A firm may have the best compensation plan and training program in the industry, but if it cannot attract qualified candidates into the pipeline, none of it matters. Recruitment is not a matter of posting a job and waiting; it is a competitive marketing activity. The firms that win the talent war (Google, Amazon, Goldman Sachs for their respective tiers) invest heavily in employer branding, campus presence, referral networks, and candidate experience. Others (hospitals, nursing homes, long-haul trucking) struggle with chronic shortages because they have not built credible recruitment capabilities or their employee value proposition is weaker than competitors. The pandemic reshaped recruitment further: remote work removed geographic constraints for many roles, increasing both opportunity and competition. Salary transparency laws in New York, California, and Colorado require employers to post pay ranges, forcing honest conversations about pay philosophy. Meanwhile, LinkedIn has become the primary recruiting platform for professional roles, and TikTok is reshaping how Gen Z finds jobs. Effective recruitment in this environment requires data (know which sources produce the best hires), investment (employer branding is expensive but compounds), and candidate experience (ghosting candidates damages the brand and reduces future pipeline).
key_concepts: Workforce planning: forecasting future human resource needs and gaps based on strategy, attrition, demographics, and changing job requirements
Recruitment yield pyramid: the ratio at each stage of the funnel (applications, qualified candidates, interviewed, offered, accepted, hired); diagnosing where the pipeline leaks guides improvement

Internal recruitment: job posting to existing employees, skills inventories, succession planning; advantages include knowing the candidate and demonstrating career paths; disadvantages include inbreeding and leaving a vacancy in the source job

External recruitment sources: employee referrals (often the highest quality), college recruiting, job boards (Indeed, LinkedIn, specialized boards), executive search firms, professional associations, social media

Employee referrals: typically produce higher-quality hires with better retention and lower cost than other sources; research shows referrals are 40% more likely to still be with the firm after two years

Realistic job preview (RJP): providing candidates with accurate information about both positive and negative aspects of the job; reduces early turnover by screening out those who would leave once reality set in
Employer brand: the reputation of the firm as a place to work; built through current employee experience, Glassdoor and similar sites, press coverage, and deliberate marketing
Employee value proposition (EVP): the bundle of benefits, pay, development, culture, and work environment offered in exchange for work; must be distinctive and credible
Recruitment metrics: time to fill, cost per hire, source of hire, source yield, quality of hire (often measured by first-year performance rating and retention), offer acceptance rate
Pay transparency laws: a growing number of jurisdictions require posting salary ranges; affects recruitment marketing and has exposed internal pay inequities
Diversity recruitment: targeted outreach to underrepresented groups through HBCUs, Hispanic Scholarship Fund, Society of Women Engineers, and similar channels; part of broader DEI strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Source analysis exercise. A firm hires 60 engineers per year from 1,800 applications. Broken down by source: referrals (300 apps, 30 hires, 10% yield), job boards (800 apps, 12 hires, 1.5% yield), college recruiting (400 apps, 15 hires, 3.75% yield), executive search (50 apps, 3 hires, 6% yield). Compute cost per hire if referral bonus = $2,000, job boards = $10,000 per year total, college recruiting = $75,000 per year total, executive search = 25% of first-year salary at $150K. Referrals: $60K referral bonuses + HR time = ~$2,300/hire. Job boards: $10K/12 = $833/hire. College: $75K/15 = $5,000/hire. Search: $37.5K per hire. Now overlay quality: referrals and college show 85% first-year retention vs. 70% for job boards; executive search 90%. Compute quality-adjusted cost per retained hire. Conclusion: job boards look cheap until quality is factored in; referrals look expensive until quality and yield are considered. Shift budget toward highest quality-adjusted sources.
setup_requirements: LinkedIn Recruiter and Indeed for source analysis

review.key: Measure Sources by Quality, Not Volume
review.value: A source that produces 1000 unqualified applications is worse than one that produces 10 qualified; cost per hire must be quality-adjusted to reflect retention and performance
Referrals consistently outperform other sources on quality and retention; investing in referral programs is one of the most cost-effective recruitment moves a firm can make

Reputation as a great place to work takes years to build and minutes to damage; firms that treat candidates and employees well reap recruitment benefits for years; those that do not pay in chronic hiring difficulty
Pay transparency, Glassdoor reviews, and social media mean candidates have unprecedented information; honest employers benefit, manipulative ones get caught

learning_objectives: Define reliability and validity and explain why both matter for selection decisions
Compare the predictive validity of different selection methods (interviews, tests, work samples, assessment centers)
Design and conduct structured behavioral interviews
Apply utility analysis to evaluate the financial value of improved selection
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 6-7
Noe et al., Fundamentals of HRM (10th ed.), Ch. 6
Schmidt & Hunter, 'The Validity and Utility of Selection Methods in Personnel Psychology: Practical and Theoretical Implications of 85 Years of Research Findings,' Psychological Bulletin (1998)
McDaniel et al., 'The Validity of Employment Interviews: A Comprehensive Review and Meta-Analysis,' Journal of Applied Psychology (1994)

lecture_notes.introduction: Selection is arguably the highest-leverage HR activity: a single hiring decision can create or destroy millions of dollars of value over the employee's tenure. Research by Frank Schmidt and John Hunter synthesizing 85 years of validation studies provides definitive answers about what works and what does not. General mental ability (GMA) tests have a validity coefficient of approximately 0.51 for predicting job performance across occupations, work sample tests 0.54, structured interviews 0.51, and integrity tests 0.41. At the other extreme, years of experience (0.18), years of education (0.10), and unstructured interviews (0.33 at best, much worse in practice due to rater bias) are much weaker predictors yet remain the most commonly used methods. This gap between evidence and practice is one of the most costly in all of management: firms systematically use worse selection methods than they could, hiring worse employees than they could, paying more for worse work. Google's analytics team found that their brainteaser interviews predicted nothing about job performance and abolished them. Unilever replaced traditional resumes and interviews for entry-level hiring with online games and AI video interviews, claiming faster hiring, broader candidate pools, and better diversity. The tools exist; the challenge is organizational discipline to use them. This session develops the evidence base for selection and provides the toolkit for designing interviews and tests that actually predict job performance.
key_concepts: Reliability: consistency of measurement; a reliable test produces similar scores across repeated administrations; measured by test-retest, alternate forms, or internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha)

Validity: the extent to which a selection method measures what it claims to measure and predicts job performance; three main types: content, criterion-related (predictive or concurrent), and construct

Validity coefficient: the correlation between the selection method and job performance; values typically range from 0 to 0.5+; higher is better but a correlation of 0.5 still leaves substantial unexplained variance
General mental ability (GMA) tests: the most valid single predictor of job performance across occupations (r ~ 0.51); controversial due to adverse impact on some racial minorities

Work sample tests: candidates perform tasks similar to the job (e.g., a coding test for developers, a role-play for managers); high validity (r ~ 0.54) and high face validity; limited by cost and applicability to training-intensive jobs

Structured behavioral interview: standardized questions asking candidates about past behavior in relevant situations (STAR format: Situation, Task, Action, Result); validity ~0.51 for structured, ~0.38 for unstructured
Situational interview: presents hypothetical scenarios and asks what the candidate would do; validity comparable to behavioral interviews; useful when candidates lack relevant experience
Assessment centers: multi-method evaluation including simulations, role-plays, presentations, and tests, often over 1-2 days; high validity (~0.37) but expensive, used for management selection
Personality tests: Big Five dimensions (OCEAN) have modest validity; Conscientiousness (~0.22) is the most consistently predictive; fake-ability is a concern in high-stakes settings
Integrity tests: predict counterproductive work behaviors (theft, absenteeism) with validity around 0.41; widely used in retail, banking, and other high-trust settings

Utility analysis: converts improved selection validity into dollar value using the Brogden-Cronbach-Gleser formula; shows that even modest validity improvements produce substantial gains in large organizations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a structured behavioral interview for a sales manager role. Start from the job description and identify 5 critical competencies: relationship building, coaching, strategic thinking, accountability, and resilience. For each competency, write one behavioral question following STAR format: 'Tell me about a time you helped a struggling salesperson turn around their performance. What was the situation, what did you do, and what was the result?' Develop a behaviorally anchored rating scale with specific examples at 1 (poor), 3 (acceptable), and 5 (excellent) levels. Practice scoring three sample answers. Discuss why this approach reduces bias compared to unstructured interviews: everyone is asked the same questions, scored on the same scale, and evaluated against job-related criteria.
setup_requirements: Case packet: 'Hiring at Google: Project Oxygen'

review.value: Structured interviews consistently outperform unstructured by double the validity; the investment to design structure pays back many times over in better hires and reduced legal exposure
If a candidate can do the actual work, have them do a sample; it is the most defensible and most predictive selection method available
Multiple valid methods in combination (GMA plus structured interview plus work sample) exceed any single method; incremental validity is modest but meaningful in aggregate
A validity increase from 0.30 to 0.50 in a firm hiring 100 engineers per year translates to millions of dollars in productivity over their tenure; utility analysis makes this visible to executives

learning_objectives: Conduct a training needs analysis at the organizational, task, and person levels
Apply principles of adult learning and transfer of training
Evaluate training programs using the Kirkpatrick four-level model
Design career development and succession planning systems
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 8
Noe, Employee Training and Development (9th ed.), Ch. 3-6
Kirkpatrick & Kirkpatrick, Evaluating Training Programs (3rd ed.)
Baldwin & Ford, 'Transfer of Training: A Review and Directions for Future Research,' Personnel Psychology (1988)

lecture_notes.introduction: U.S. employers spend over $100 billion per year on training and development, yet research consistently shows that most of this investment produces little lasting change in behavior or performance. The problem is not training itself but training disconnected from real job demands, delivered in ways that do not support transfer, and evaluated by measures that do not link to outcomes. When training is done well, the return is enormous: Target, Amazon, and Costco all invest heavily in frontline employee training and attribute lower turnover, better customer service, and higher productivity to that investment. The U.S. Army's training system, refined over decades, produces reliably competent soldiers from extraordinarily diverse civilian backgrounds in a matter of months. Google's manager training following Project Oxygen translated research findings into practical workshops and tied manager behaviors to measurable outcomes. At the other extreme, the annual compliance training that most employees click through while watching something else on another screen represents pure waste: it neither changes behavior nor provides meaningful legal defense. The difference between high-return and wasted training is not the topic but the design: needs analysis that identifies real skill gaps, learning principles that match how adults learn, workplace support that enables transfer, and evaluation that holds the training accountable for outcomes.
key_concepts: Training needs analysis (TNA): systematic assessment of what training is needed; conducted at three levels - organizational (is training the right solution for the strategic challenge?), task (what knowledge and skills does the job require?), and person (which employees need which training?)
ADDIE model: Analysis, Design, Development, Implementation, Evaluation; the standard instructional design framework for building training programs
Adult learning principles (Knowles' andragogy): adults need to know why they are learning, draw on experience, are problem-centered, and are internally motivated; training design should reflect these
Transfer of training: the degree to which trainees apply training content on the job; research shows 10-40% of training content is typically transferred, meaning most training value is lost

Baldwin & Ford transfer model: trainee characteristics (motivation, ability), training design (learning principles, overlearning, relevance), and work environment (supervisor support, opportunity to practice) all affect transfer

Kirkpatrick's four levels of training evaluation: Level 1 Reaction (did they like it?), Level 2 Learning (did they learn?), Level 3 Behavior (are they doing it differently on the job?), Level 4 Results (did it improve business outcomes?); higher levels are more meaningful but harder to measure
On-the-job training (OJT): learning by doing the actual work under supervision; cheap and directly relevant but quality depends on the trainer
Formal training methods: classroom, e-learning, simulations, role-plays, case studies, coaching; each suited to different content types
Career development: structured support for employees to advance over time; includes career paths, mentoring, stretch assignments, and tuition reimbursement
Succession planning: identifying and developing employees for key future positions; protects against leadership gaps and signals career opportunity to high performers

70-20-10 rule: popular heuristic that 70% of learning comes from on-the-job experience, 20% from relationships and coaching, 10% from formal training; research is mixed but the emphasis on experiential learning is correct
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a training program for new customer service representatives at a hotel chain. Conduct task analysis: greeting guests, handling check-in, resolving complaints, processing payments, communicating with housekeeping. Conduct person analysis: new hires have customer service experience but lack system knowledge and chain-specific procedures. Design 5-day program: Day 1 classroom (company culture, service standards, brand values); Day 2 technical (property management system, POS, key systems) with hands-on practice; Day 3 role-plays of common guest interactions; Day 4 shadowing experienced employees; Day 5 supervised live shifts with coach feedback. Plan evaluation at all four Kirkpatrick levels: reaction (end-of-week survey), learning (system certification test, role-play assessment), behavior (supervisor observations at 30 and 90 days), results (guest satisfaction scores, first-call resolution rate). Discuss how this differs from typical 'orientation' that covers policies without building capability.

review.value: Training that is not grounded in a careful needs analysis solves the wrong problem or misses the real problem entirely; the most common reason training fails is that it was never needed
Training that does not transfer to behavior on the job is wasted investment regardless of how well-designed the class was; workplace support is as important as training design

Most training evaluation stops at Level 1 (did they like it?) which is almost meaningless; pushing to Level 3 (behavior change) and Level 4 (business results) is hard but that is where the value is proven
Experience, stretch assignments, and coaching develop people more than classroom training; firms that focus only on formal training miss the biggest levers for capability building

learning_objectives: Design a performance management system that aligns individual performance with strategy
Compare appraisal methods: graphic rating scales, BARS, MBO, 360-degree feedback
Identify and mitigate common rater errors and biases
Conduct an effective performance feedback conversation
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 9
Noe et al., Fundamentals of HRM (10th ed.), Ch. 8
Pulakos, Performance Management (SIOP) (2009)
Buckingham & Goodall, 'Reinventing Performance Management,' Harvard Business Review (2015)

lecture_notes.introduction: Performance management is the most disliked HR process, by managers and employees alike. Surveys consistently find that only about 30% of employees believe their firm's performance management system actually helps them improve, and most managers consider annual reviews a time drain that produces little value. Yet firms that have abandoned performance management entirely have generally regretted it, and those that have redesigned it carefully (GE, Adobe, Microsoft, Deloitte) have seen improvements in engagement and performance. The challenge is that performance management must simultaneously accomplish goals that are often in tension: evaluating past performance for pay and promotion decisions, providing feedback that drives improvement, aligning individual work with strategy, identifying high potentials, and documenting problems that may lead to termination. Systems optimized for one purpose (e.g., legal defensibility) often fail at others (e.g., coaching for improvement). The classic annual review, with forced ranking and extensive paperwork, was widely adopted in the 20th century and is now widely discredited as counterproductive. Modern approaches emphasize frequent informal conversations, forward-looking coaching, and outcome-based measurement tied to clear goals. The best systems are simple, trust line managers to use judgment, and focus on the future more than the past. The worst systems are complex, bureaucratic, and turn performance management into a compliance exercise that everyone hates.
key_concepts: Performance management: the continuous process of identifying, measuring, and developing individual and team performance and aligning it with strategic goals; more than annual appraisal
Purposes of performance management: strategic alignment, administrative decisions (pay, promotion, termination), development and feedback, documentation, workforce planning
Graphic rating scales: rate employees on dimensions (quality, productivity, reliability) using a numerical scale; simple but prone to vague definitions and rater errors
Behaviorally anchored rating scales (BARS): rating scales with specific behavioral examples at each score level; harder to build but more objective and reliable

Management by objectives (MBO): cascading goals from organizational to individual level; rates performance on goal achievement; works well for results-oriented jobs but less well where goals are ambiguous
360-degree feedback: input from self, peers, subordinates, and supervisor; useful for development but controversial for administrative decisions due to inflation and political gaming

Forced ranking (stack ranking): requires managers to distribute employees into fixed categories (e.g., top 20%, middle 70%, bottom 10%); GE popularized it, Microsoft famously abandoned it after years of culture damage

Rater errors: halo (one trait influences others), leniency (tendency to rate high), severity (tendency to rate low), central tendency (avoiding extremes), recency (overweighting recent events), similar-to-me bias
Calibration sessions: managers meet to discuss ratings and normalize standards; reduce rater errors and inflation but require time and candor
Continuous performance management: replaces annual reviews with ongoing check-ins, real-time feedback, and lightweight documentation; adopted by Adobe, Deloitte, Accenture since 2012

Feedback conversations: research shows feedback that is specific, timely, behavior-focused (not personality), and balanced produces the best outcomes; avoid the 'feedback sandwich' which dilutes the message
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performance management redesign case. A regional hospital chain conducts annual reviews rated on a 5-point scale with managers spending an average of 8 hours per direct report per year (including forms, meetings, calibration). Employee engagement surveys show only 22% believe the review helps them improve. HR proposes replacing annual reviews with quarterly conversations, a simpler 3-point system (exceeds, meets, below), and removal of forced ranking. Opponents argue this will lose the signal needed for pay and promotion decisions. Walk through the design: quarterly 30-minute manager-employee conversations focused on goal progress, obstacles, and development; simplified documentation; annual calibration session for pay recommendations; separation of coaching conversations from rating conversations. Discuss risk: without forced ranking, rating inflation is a concern, so calibration discipline is critical. Estimated time savings: 4 hours per employee per year, freeing managers for actual coaching.
setup_requirements: Video: 'Radical Candor' by Kim Scott on feedback

review.value: When rating consequences are attached to every conversation, honest dialogue disappears; high-performing systems separate informal coaching (ongoing) from formal rating (periodic)
Common rater errors degrade ratings even with well-designed forms; training raters on frame of reference, observation, and documentation improves reliability substantially

'You need to communicate better' is useless; 'In last week's client meeting, you interrupted twice and spoke over the client when they raised concerns about timing' is actionable and improvement-focused

The most sophisticated performance management systems often produce the worst outcomes because managers game the system rather than use it; simpler systems trusted to line managers typically produce better results

learning_objectives: Integrate staffing, development, and performance concepts into coherent HR system design
Diagnose HR problems using data and identify root causes
Communicate HR recommendations with business impact estimates
Prepare for midterm examination covering Weeks 1-7
readings: Review: Dessler, Chapters 1-9; Noe, Chapters 1-8
Case: 'SAS Institute: A Different Approach to Incentives and People Management' (HBS 9-498-007)
Case: 'Lincoln Electric: Venturing Abroad' (HBS 9-398-095)

lecture_notes.introduction: HR systems work as coherent bundles; changing one element without considering its interaction with others usually fails. SAS Institute (the analytics software firm) built a legendary workplace over four decades on a coherent HR philosophy: selective hiring, heavy investment in employees (on-site health care, daycare, fitness center, 35-hour workweek), a paternalistic culture, and an emphasis on long tenure rather than high-performance turnover. This worked because all the pieces reinforced each other: the benefits attracted stable employees, the stable employees built deep expertise, the expertise produced the software SAS sold, and the profits funded the benefits. Trying to implement SAS's benefits package at a firm with a Silicon Valley turnover culture would fail because the pieces would not fit. Lincoln Electric built an equally coherent but opposite system: piece-rate compensation, no guaranteed employment at hire, year-end bonuses based on individual productivity and quality, and an intensely meritocratic culture. Lincoln's system has produced the highest productivity per employee in its industry for nearly a century in the United States, but the firm struggled to replicate it abroad where local cultures did not accept the extreme pay variability. The lesson is that HR strategy must be internally coherent and locally appropriate; cookie-cutter copying of practices without understanding why they work usually fails.
key_concepts: HR bundles: groups of practices that reinforce each other; the whole is greater than the sum of parts because practices support each other
Internal fit (consistency): practices within HR reinforce rather than contradict (e.g., selective hiring with heavy training, not either alone)
External fit (alignment): HR practices align with business strategy (e.g., innovation strategy demands different HR than cost leadership)

SAS Institute: high-investment model built on selective hiring, rich benefits, work-life balance, long tenure; works because the pieces reinforce each other and the business model rewards deep expertise

Lincoln Electric: high-variable-pay model built on piece-rate compensation, year-end bonuses, no guaranteed employment; works in U.S. manufacturing culture but fails when transplanted to cultures with different pay norms

Problem diagnosis: HR symptoms (high turnover, low engagement) often have multiple possible causes (compensation, management, job design, selection, culture); thorough diagnosis avoids treating symptoms instead of causes
Root cause analysis: 5 Whys or fishbone diagrams help trace symptoms to actionable causes
Business case for HR changes: effective HR recommendations quantify costs and expected benefits; executives need to see the business impact, not just the HR rationale
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case diagnosis exercise using SAS. A competitor of SAS wants to replicate SAS's success by adopting its benefits package (on-site amenities, long vacations, 35-hour week). Walk through why this would likely fail without adopting the rest of the SAS system: selective hiring standards, culture of deep expertise, long tenure expectation, paternalistic management style, business model that rewards long tenure. Isolated adoption of expensive benefits would drain resources without producing the productivity gains that finance them. Contrast with Lincoln Electric's failed attempts to export its piece-rate compensation system to European operations where local labor laws and cultural norms rejected it. Lessons for HR strategy: coherence matters more than individual best practices.
setup_requirements: SAS and Lincoln Electric case packets
Midterm review sheet with concept summary
Business case template for HR recommendations

review.value: Copying individual practices from high-performing firms without copying the system that makes them work usually fails; diagnose what makes the system coherent before recommending changes

review.key: HR Follows Strategy, Not Vice Versa
review.value: A cost leader needs different HR than a differentiator; HR practices that fit one strategy may actively damage another, so HR design should start from strategic clarity
Hiring, firing, compensating, and promoting differently can change HR structures quickly, but the culture they produce takes years to develop; patience is required
Global HR systems must be adapted to local labor markets, laws, and cultures; what motivates a Cleveland welder may not motivate a Mumbai engineer, and assuming otherwise invites failure

learning_objectives: Define compensation strategy elements: internal alignment, external competitiveness, employee contributions
Conduct job evaluation using point method and create pay grades
Analyze market pay surveys and establish pay ranges
Evaluate pay strategies including lead, lag, and match approaches
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 11-12
Milkovich, Newman & Gerhart, Compensation (13th ed.), Ch. 1-5
Gerhart & Rynes, Compensation: Theory, Evidence, and Strategic Implications (2003)
Cascio, 'The High Cost of Low Wages,' Harvard Business Review (2006)

lecture_notes.introduction: Compensation is the largest HR cost category for most firms, often 50-70% of total operating expense in service businesses, and the element of HR that employees pay closest attention to. Compensation decisions also have the strongest legal exposure: the Equal Pay Act (1963), Title VII, and state pay equity laws create liability for pay discrimination that can run into hundreds of millions of dollars. Getting compensation wrong damages recruitment (pay too little), undermines profitability (pay too much), breeds inequity (inconsistent structures), or incentivizes the wrong behavior (rewarding quantity while expecting quality). The classic debate pits Costco's high-wage strategy (paying well above Walmart's Sam's Club) against the traditional retail low-wage approach. Costco's wages produce lower turnover, higher productivity, better customer service, and cost savings that partially offset the pay premium. Wayne Cascio's research showed that Costco's total labor cost per dollar of sales was actually comparable to Sam's Club despite the higher wages, because turnover and training costs were lower. Similarly, the In-N-Out Burger chain pays above the industry norm and enjoys legendary employee retention and customer loyalty. The lesson is not that high wages are always right, but that labor cost is about productivity, retention, and quality, not just the wage number. This session develops the tools to design compensation structures that balance internal equity, external competitiveness, and employee motivation.
key_concepts: Compensation strategy: the pattern of pay decisions that supports the firm's business strategy; includes level (how much), structure (internal differentials), and form (mix of pay types)
Three dimensions of compensation: internal alignment (relative pay for jobs within the firm), external competitiveness (pay relative to labor market), employee contributions (pay for performance)
Total rewards: base pay + variable pay + benefits + non-monetary rewards (career development, recognition, work environment); the full value proposition to employees
Job evaluation: systematic process of establishing relative worth of jobs for pay purposes; methods include ranking, classification, point method, and factor comparison
Point method: rate jobs on compensable factors (skill, effort, responsibility, working conditions) using weighted points; sums produce job scores that determine pay grade
Pay grades: groupings of jobs of similar evaluated worth; reduces administrative complexity while maintaining rough equity
Pay ranges: minimum, midpoint, and maximum for each grade; midpoint usually set at market median; range width allows progression within grade without promotion
Compa-ratio: individual pay divided by range midpoint; tracks pay progression and identifies outliers
Market surveys: salary data from peer firms, by job and location; sources include Mercer, Radford, Willis Towers Watson, PayScale, Salary.com; crucial for external competitiveness
Pay strategy: lead (pay above market, attract top talent), match (pay at market, balanced approach), lag (pay below market, rely on non-monetary benefits or accept higher turnover)
Compression: when new hires or external employees earn similar pay to long-tenured employees due to market increases; creates dissatisfaction and turnover of tenured staff
Pay equity: legal requirement that men and women (and other protected groups) receive equal pay for equal work; requires regular audits and corrective action
lecture_notes.demonstration: Job evaluation exercise using the point method. Evaluate three jobs: (1) Customer service representative, (2) Accountant, (3) Warehouse supervisor. Compensable factors: skill (max 400 points), effort (max 200), responsibility (max 300), working conditions (max 100). Rate each job on each factor using benchmarks. CSR: skill 180, effort 120, responsibility 150, conditions 60, total 510. Accountant: skill 320, effort 150, responsibility 240, conditions 30, total 740. Warehouse supervisor: skill 220, effort 180, responsibility 260, conditions 80, total 740. Establish pay grades: 400-499, 500-599, 600-699, 700-799, 800-899, 900+. Accountant and warehouse supervisor both in 700-799 grade despite being very different jobs (internally equitable by this measure). Check against market data: if market median for accountants is $60K and warehouse supervisors is $55K, need to decide whether internal equity (same grade) or external competitiveness (different pay) takes precedence. Discuss when to override job evaluation with market data.
setup_requirements: Case packet: 'Costco: Cheapest, Happiest Company in the World'

review.value: High-variable-pay structures support entrepreneurial cultures; steady-base-pay structures support stable cultures; neither is universally right, and the alignment of pay with strategy matters more than the pay level itself

review.key: Internal Equity and Market Competitiveness Tension
review.value: Rigorous job evaluation creates internal equity, but market forces may push some jobs above or below their evaluated worth; firms must decide which dimension dominates and accept the trade-offs

review.key: Total Labor Cost, Not Wage Rate
review.value: A higher wage rate can produce lower total labor cost when it reduces turnover, improves productivity, and raises customer satisfaction; Costco and In-N-Out are proof of concept
Pay discrimination claims can originate in legitimate individual decisions that compound into systematic disparities; regular equity audits catch drift before it becomes litigation

learning_objectives: Describe the major categories of employee benefits and their strategic role
Design incentive pay programs at individual, team, and organizational levels
Apply principles of pay for performance including expectancy theory and agency theory
Evaluate health insurance, retirement, and other major benefit programs
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 13
Milkovich et al., Compensation (13th ed.), Ch. 9-12
Lazear, 'Performance Pay and Productivity,' American Economic Review (2000)
Rosen, 'The Theory of Equalizing Differences,' in Handbook of Labor Economics

lecture_notes.introduction: Benefits now average about 30% of total compensation cost in the United States, making them a major financial commitment and a major driver of employee decisions. Health insurance alone costs employers an average of $16,000 per employee per year for family coverage and continues rising faster than general inflation. Retirement benefits have shifted dramatically from defined benefit pensions (guaranteed retirement income) to defined contribution plans like 401(k)s (employee-directed investment) over the past 40 years, transferring risk from employers to workers. Paid leave, family benefits, tuition assistance, wellness programs, and increasingly mental health support all signal employer priorities and influence recruitment, retention, and engagement. Incentive pay is the other half of the variable compensation picture: sales commissions, individual bonuses, team bonuses, profit sharing, and equity compensation all attempt to align employee behavior with firm outcomes. Edward Lazear's famous Safelite Auto Glass study measured a 44% productivity increase when the firm shifted from hourly pay to piece-rate pay for glass installers, dramatically validating the power of incentive design. But incentives are notoriously vulnerable to gaming: Wells Fargo's account-opening incentive program led thousands of employees to create millions of fraudulent accounts, producing one of the largest scandals in banking history. Designing incentives that produce desired behavior without destructive side effects requires both economic theory (expectancy, agency) and practical wisdom about what can go wrong.
key_concepts: Legally required benefits: Social Security, Medicare, unemployment insurance, workers compensation, family and medical leave (FMLA job protection); employers have no discretion
Voluntary benefits: health insurance, retirement plans, paid time off, life and disability insurance, tuition assistance, childcare, wellness programs; variable across firms
Health insurance: employer-sponsored coverage for 155+ million Americans; costs rising 5-7% annually; high-deductible plans with HSAs increasingly common to manage costs
Retirement plans: defined benefit (pension) promises a specific monthly amount, firm bears investment risk; defined contribution (401(k)) depends on contributions and returns, employee bears risk
Paid time off: vacation, sick leave, personal days, holidays; increasingly consolidated into unified PTO banks; some firms offer unlimited PTO (which research shows often results in less time taken)
Cafeteria (flexible benefits) plans: Section 125 plans allowing employees to choose benefits from a menu with pre-tax dollars; tailors benefits to individual needs

Expectancy theory (Vroom): motivation = expectancy (effort leads to performance) times instrumentality (performance leads to reward) times valence (reward has value); all three must be present for incentive pay to motivate
Agency theory: when owners (principals) hire workers (agents), interests diverge; incentive pay aligns the agent's interests with the principal's by tying pay to desired outcomes
Individual incentives: piece rates, sales commissions, merit increases, spot bonuses; clearest line of sight but can undermine teamwork
Team and group incentives: team bonuses, gainsharing (Scanlon, Improshare, Rucker plans), profit sharing; encourage cooperation but dilute individual accountability
Equity compensation: stock options, restricted stock, employee stock purchase plans; ties employees to long-term firm value; common in tech and executive compensation

Gaming incentive plans: employees optimize measured metrics at expense of unmeasured outcomes (teaching to the test); Wells Fargo, Sears auto repair, British Rail on-time statistics are cautionary examples
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design an incentive plan for a regional sales team of 15 representatives at a business software company. Sales cycle is 6-9 months, team collaboration is important (representatives share leads and help close each other's deals), and products require deep technical knowledge. Option A: pure individual commission (straight 10% of personal sales). Option B: 70% individual commission + 30% team bonus based on total team sales. Option C: 50% base salary + 25% individual commission + 25% team bonus with quality modifier (customer retention after 1 year). Walk through pros and cons: A maximizes individual effort but may destroy collaboration; B balances individual and team but dilutes individual signal; C adds quality incentive but is more complex. Apply expectancy theory: which option best links effort to performance to reward? Apply agency theory: which option best aligns with firm goals (new sales, retention, customer success)? Recommend C with specific targets and discuss measurement risks.
setup_requirements: Case packet: 'Incentive Plans at Lincoln Electric' and 'Wells Fargo Account Scandal'

review.key: Design for What You Want to Measure
review.value: Incentives produce what they measure, not what you want; if the metric does not fully capture the desired outcome, employees will optimize the metric at the expense of the outcome
For incentives to motivate, employees must see how their effort affects the metric; plans tied to outcomes employees cannot influence produce no motivation, just lottery-like pay variability

Rich health benefits attract workers with families and health concerns; strong retirement plans attract older workers; tuition assistance attracts younger workers; benefits design signals who you want
Every incentive can be gamed; smart design includes quality modifiers, audits, and hard caps on individual pay-outs to limit the damage when gaming occurs

learning_objectives: Describe the structure of U.S. labor law including the NLRA, LMRA, and LMRDA
Explain the union organizing and certification process
Describe the collective bargaining process and major contract provisions
Analyze recent trends in union membership and labor action
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 15
Noe et al., Fundamentals of HRM (10th ed.), Ch. 14
Fossum, Labor Relations: Development, Structure, Process (13th ed.), Ch. 1-6
Wagner Act (NLRA 1935), Taft-Hartley Act (LMRA 1947) - primary texts

lecture_notes.introduction: U.S. union membership has declined from 35% of the workforce in the 1950s to about 10% today, with private-sector membership at roughly 6%. Yet 2022-2024 saw a resurgence of interest in unions: Amazon warehouse workers in Staten Island won the first successful unionization at the company, Starbucks baristas organized over 400 stores, and the United Auto Workers conducted a successful 46-day strike against Detroit's Big Three automakers that won substantial wage increases and restored cost-of-living adjustments. Public approval of unions reached 71% in 2023 polls, the highest since the 1960s. Whether or not any individual firm is unionized, understanding labor relations is essential for HR professionals: labor law shapes employment practices for both unionized and non-union workers, the threat of unionization constrains how non-union firms manage employees, and multi-employer collective bargaining shapes entire industries. The U.S. labor relations system, built on the Wagner Act of 1935 (NLRA) and its amendments, creates a particular balance between worker rights to organize and employer rights to resist, with the National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) as referee. The system differs substantially from European co-determination models and from the near-total prohibition of unions in parts of Asia and the Middle East. For HR, the key questions are: what are the legal rules of organizing, what does collective bargaining actually look like, and how should firms manage the employee relationship whether or not a union is present.
key_concepts: National Labor Relations Act (Wagner Act, 1935): established the right of employees to form unions and bargain collectively; prohibited unfair labor practices by employers; created the NLRB

Labor-Management Relations Act (Taft-Hartley, 1947): amended NLRA to prohibit unfair labor practices by unions; permitted state right-to-work laws; allowed Presidential injunction of strikes threatening national health or safety
Landrum-Griffin Act (LMRDA, 1959): regulated internal union affairs including union elections, financial reporting, and member rights
NLRB (National Labor Relations Board): federal agency that conducts union representation elections and adjudicates unfair labor practice charges

Union organizing process: authorization cards signed by 30%+ of workers triggers NLRB election petition; 50%+ vote in secret ballot wins certification; union becomes exclusive bargaining representative
Unfair labor practices by employers (Section 8a): interfering with organizing, dominating unions, discriminating against union members, refusing to bargain in good faith
Collective bargaining: negotiation between union and employer over wages, hours, and terms of employment; results in a collective bargaining agreement (contract) typically 3-5 years
Mandatory bargaining subjects: wages, hours, and other terms and conditions of employment; must be negotiated if raised
Permissive bargaining subjects: not required but may be negotiated if both parties agree
Illegal bargaining subjects: closed shop (requiring union membership for hire), discriminatory clauses; cannot be negotiated
Contract provisions: wages and benefits, grievance procedure, seniority, job classifications, management rights, no-strike/no-lockout, union security (shop clauses)
Strike: organized work stoppage by employees; economic strikes (for better terms) vs. unfair labor practice strikes (with stronger legal protection)
Right-to-work laws: state laws prohibiting union security agreements that require union membership or fees as condition of employment; adopted by 27 states as of recent years
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis of a union organizing campaign. Workers at a regional hospital begin gathering signatures on authorization cards. Management becomes aware and must decide how to respond within the legal framework. Illegal responses (interrogation, threats, promises of benefits contingent on rejecting union, surveillance of union activities) would expose the hospital to unfair labor practice charges. Legal responses (express management opinion through captive audience meetings, distribute information about unions, highlight current benefits, correct misinformation) are permitted and widely used. Walk through the timeline: card collection, NLRB petition at 30% support, NLRB conducts election typically 6-8 weeks later, vote counted by NLRB officer. Discuss what makes unions appealing (job security, collective voice, better wages) and how non-union firms prevent organizing by addressing those concerns directly (fair treatment, competitive pay, open communication).
setup_requirements: NLRA and Taft-Hartley primary text excerpts
Case packet: 'Amazon Warehouse Unionization in Staten Island'
NLRB election petition forms and procedures

review.value: Research consistently shows workers form unions because they perceive unfair treatment and lack of voice; firms that treat employees well and listen to concerns rarely face successful organizing campaigns
The NLRA constrains employer responses to organizing; violations (threats, promises, surveillance) can result in the election being set aside and the union certified even if it lost the vote

Bargaining is not a free-form negotiation; mandatory subjects, good-faith obligations, and specific procedures shape the process; HR professionals in unionized environments must understand these rules

The threat of unionization shapes HR practices at non-union firms; employment laws (minimum wage, overtime, safety) apply universally; understanding labor relations is essential for all HR professionals

class_schedule.topic: Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Employee Engagement
learning_objectives: Define diversity, equity, inclusion, and belonging and explain their business case
Design DEI programs and evaluate their effectiveness
Measure and improve employee engagement using validated instruments
Identify barriers to inclusion including unconscious bias and structural factors
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 2 (EEO) and Ch. 17
Page, The Diversity Bonus: How Great Teams Pay Off in the Knowledge Economy (2017)
Dobbin & Kalev, 'Why Diversity Programs Fail,' Harvard Business Review (2016)
Gallup, State of the Global Workplace Report (annual)

lecture_notes.introduction: Diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) has become one of the most contested areas of HR practice. The business case for diversity rests on both moral grounds and empirical research: McKinsey's Diversity Wins reports consistently find positive correlations between executive diversity and financial performance; Scott Page's research shows diverse teams solve complex problems more effectively than homogeneous ones; and firms with inclusive cultures report higher engagement, retention, and innovation. Yet DEI programs have a mixed track record: Dobbin and Kalev's research published in HBR analyzed three decades of data from hundreds of firms and found that mandatory diversity training, bias training, and grievance procedures often backfired, while mentoring, cross-functional task forces, and diversity managers produced measurable gains. The lesson is that DEI success requires rigorous program design grounded in evidence, not well-intentioned programs copied from other firms. At the same time, employee engagement (the emotional commitment employees feel toward their work and organization) has emerged as the single most important HR outcome metric. Gallup's research shows that only 23% of employees worldwide are engaged, and disengaged employees cost the global economy an estimated $8.8 trillion in lost productivity. Engaged workforces dramatically outperform disengaged ones on every business metric. Building an engaged and inclusive workforce is the goal of modern HR, and this session develops the frameworks to pursue it effectively.
key_concepts: Diversity: the presence of differences in identity, background, and perspective in a group; dimensions include demographic (race, gender, age), cognitive (thinking style, education), and experiential

Equity: fair treatment, access, opportunity, and advancement while striving to identify and eliminate barriers that prevent full participation; differs from equality by recognizing different starting points
Inclusion: creating an environment where all individuals feel welcomed, respected, supported, and valued; the subjective experience of being part of the group
Belonging: deeper sense of being accepted as one's authentic self; the affective outcome of inclusion

Business case for diversity: cognitive diversity improves problem-solving on complex tasks (Page, Hong); demographic diversity correlates with financial performance (McKinsey); but causation is debated

Unconscious bias: implicit attitudes that affect decisions without awareness; measured by Implicit Association Test (IAT); common examples include affinity bias (favoring similar others) and halo effect
Types of DEI interventions: recruiting and pipeline programs, mentoring and sponsorship, employee resource groups (ERGs), bias training, accountability systems, inclusive leadership development
Dobbin & Kalev findings: mandatory bias training and grievance procedures often backfire; voluntary training, mentoring, diversity managers, and accountability mechanisms work better
Employee engagement: the emotional commitment and discretionary effort employees bring to their work; distinct from satisfaction (which is passive)
Gallup Q12: 12-item engagement survey measuring perceptions of expectations, resources, recognition, development, voice, and relationships; predicts retention, productivity, and profitability
Drivers of engagement: meaningful work, strong management, recognition, growth opportunities, clear expectations, fit with company values, appropriate workload
Psychological safety (Amy Edmondson): a climate where people feel safe to speak up, take risks, and make mistakes; prerequisite for both inclusion and innovation
Microaggressions: brief, often unintentional slights that communicate exclusion or devaluation; cumulative effect on targets can be substantial
lecture_notes.demonstration: DEI program evaluation exercise. A firm has implemented the following programs over three years: mandatory annual bias training ($200K/year), an employee resource group program ($50K/year), diversity recruiting at HBCUs ($300K/year), and a mentorship program pairing senior leaders with underrepresented mid-level managers ($75K/year). Workforce representation has improved modestly at entry level but not at management level. Engagement scores are stable. Apply the Dobbin & Kalev framework: mandatory training likely producing backlash or no effect; ERGs modestly positive; recruiting improving pipeline; mentorship (voluntary, builds accountability) likely highest impact. Recommendation: eliminate mandatory training, expand mentorship, add sponsorship (advocacy for promotion), create manager accountability for representation. Discuss how evidence-based DEI requires measurement, willingness to abandon ineffective programs, and structural change not just awareness.
setup_requirements: Case packet: 'DEI at Johnson & Johnson' and 'Engagement at Southwest Airlines'

review.value: Well-intentioned DEI programs often fail; rigorous evaluation identifies what works (mentoring, accountability, structural change) vs. what does not (mandatory bias training, grievance procedures)
Hiring a diverse workforce is relatively straightforward; creating an environment where diverse employees thrive and contribute fully is much harder and requires cultural work

Engaged employees show higher productivity, better customer service, lower turnover, fewer safety incidents, and higher profitability; engagement is the strongest leading indicator of business performance
Gallup research consistently shows that 70% of variance in engagement is attributable to the manager; investing in manager development is the highest-leverage way to improve engagement

class_schedule.topic: HR Analytics, Metrics, and Workforce Planning
learning_objectives: Design an HR analytics function with appropriate data, skills, and governance
Compute and interpret key HR metrics including turnover, retention, and cost per hire
Apply predictive analytics to workforce planning and talent management
Evaluate the ethical and privacy considerations of HR data and AI
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 3
Davenport, Harris & Shapiro, 'Competing on Talent Analytics,' Harvard Business Review (2010)
Bersin by Deloitte, 'High-Impact People Analytics' (annual research)
Marler & Boudreau, 'An Evidence-Based Review of HR Analytics,' International Journal of HRM (2017)

lecture_notes.introduction: HR has traditionally been the least data-driven business function, relying on intuition, precedent, and anecdote rather than evidence. That is changing rapidly. Google's People Operations, the paradigm case of HR analytics, made data-driven decisions on everything from brainteaser interviews (abolished when they showed zero validity) to manager behavior (Project Oxygen identified the eight behaviors of effective managers through regression analysis). IBM, Microsoft, and Walmart have built substantial people analytics teams that apply statistical methods and machine learning to workforce questions. At the same time, HR data raises serious ethical questions: predictive turnover models can identify employees likely to leave, but acting on those predictions (without disclosing them) can feel manipulative. AI screening tools have been found to encode historical biases (Amazon famously scrapped a machine learning resume screener that penalized women). Employee monitoring software proliferates but raises privacy and trust concerns. The rise of HR analytics creates both opportunity (better decisions, fairer outcomes, measurable impact) and risk (bias, privacy violations, dehumanization). HR professionals must develop enough data fluency to participate in analytics work, ask sharp questions of data scientists, and guard against the misuse of employee data. This session introduces the major categories of HR metrics, the analytical methods applied to HR data, and the ethical principles that should guide their use.
key_concepts: HR metrics categories: efficiency (cost per hire, time to fill), effectiveness (quality of hire, training ROI), impact (turnover, engagement, productivity), and strategic (alignment with business outcomes)
Turnover metrics: annual turnover rate (separations/average headcount), voluntary vs. involuntary, regretted vs. unregretted, by tenure, by manager, by location; the most watched HR metric

Turnover cost: includes separation costs (exit interview, severance), replacement costs (recruiting, selection), training costs (onboarding, productivity ramp), and productivity loss during transition; typically 50-200% of annual salary
Quality of hire: measured by first-year performance rating, first-year retention, and manager satisfaction; ties selection to outcomes rather than just filling roles
Engagement metrics: Gallup Q12, pulse surveys, eNPS (employee Net Promoter Score); tracked over time and segmented by team
Descriptive analytics: what happened? (historical reporting)
Diagnostic analytics: why did it happen? (root cause analysis, correlation)
Predictive analytics: what will happen? (forecasting, classification models)
Prescriptive analytics: what should we do? (optimization, A/B testing)

Google Project Oxygen: analyzed 10,000 manager performance reviews and employee survey responses to identify the behaviors that separated high- from low-performing managers; tied results to manager training
People analytics team structure: typically reports to CHRO, combines HR domain knowledge with data science skills; governance critical due to sensitivity of employee data
Ethical considerations: informed consent for data collection, transparency about how data is used, avoiding discriminatory impact, balancing productivity monitoring with employee autonomy and dignity
GDPR and employee data: EU regulation treats employee data as personal data requiring consent, limitation, and right to access; U.S. increasingly following similar principles through state laws
lecture_notes.demonstration: Turnover analysis exercise. A 2,000-employee firm has an annual voluntary turnover rate of 18%. Segment the data: engineering 8%, sales 28%, customer service 35%, corporate 12%. Turnover by tenure: 0-12 months 40%, 1-3 years 22%, 3-5 years 12%, 5+ years 6%. Turnover by manager: top quartile 9%, middle quartiles 16%, bottom quartile 30%. Compute the cost of turnover using an average replacement cost of 80% of annual salary: 18% of 2000 = 360 departures, average salary $75K, cost per departure $60K, total cost = $21.6M per year. Identify priorities: (1) Customer service turnover is twice the firm rate and concentrated in first year; likely a selection, onboarding, or job quality issue. (2) Bottom-quartile managers triple-rate the top quartile; a management quality issue. (3) First-year turnover of 40% suggests selection or realistic job preview problems. Recommend targeted interventions: structured onboarding for customer service, manager coaching or reassignment for bottom quartile, realistic job previews for candidates. Estimate impact: if these interventions reduce overall turnover to 13%, savings = 5% of 2000 * $60K = $6M per year.
setup_requirements: Case packet: 'People Analytics at Google: Project Oxygen'

review.value: Aggregate HR metrics hide the patterns that matter; segmentation by manager, job family, tenure, and location reveals where to focus interventions
HR analytics projects that start with data rather than business questions produce interesting but useless findings; always begin with 'what decision does this analysis enable?'
HR datasets are observational, not experimental; findings are correlations, not causal claims; rigorous analytics distinguishes the two and avoids acting on spurious relationships
Employee data is sensitive and trust is fragile; HR analytics must be governed by explicit ethical principles about consent, transparency, fairness, and appropriate use

class_schedule.topic: International HR and Global Workforce Management
learning_objectives: Compare HR practices across countries and explain the drivers of variation
Design expatriate assignment programs including selection, preparation, and repatriation
Apply Hofstede's and other cultural frameworks to HR decisions
Evaluate the trade-offs between global standardization and local adaptation
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 17
Dowling, Festing & Engle, International Human Resource Management (7th ed.), Ch. 1-4
Hofstede, Culture's Consequences (2nd ed.)
Black, Gregersen, Mendenhall & Stroh, Globalizing People through International Assignments

lecture_notes.introduction: Managing HR across borders is substantially more complex than managing in a single country. Employment laws vary dramatically: France has a 35-hour workweek and mandatory five weeks of vacation; Germany has strong codetermination laws giving workers board seats; China has restrictions on at-will termination; the U.S. has at-will employment in most states. Cultural norms about hierarchy, individualism, uncertainty, and gender vary by country in ways that affect leadership style, compensation philosophy, performance management, and conflict resolution. Multinational firms must decide how much to standardize practices globally vs. adapt them to local conditions. McDonald's enforces strict operational standards globally but adapts HR practices (compensation, hours, benefits) to local markets. Google tries to maintain consistent culture globally but accepts significant local variation in pay and benefits. Walmart famously failed in Germany partly because it tried to impose U.S. HR practices (chanting morning cheers, smiling at customers, banning workplace romance) on a culture that rejected them. Expatriate assignments (sending employees from one country to work in another) are a particular challenge: success rates for expatriates have historically been poor, with early return rates of 20-40% in traditional programs, and even successful assignments often fail to produce expected development or knowledge transfer.
