This session develops the frameworks to understand cultural differences, design expatriate programs, and decide what to standardize globally vs. localize.
key_concepts: International HRM: managing people in multinational firms across national and cultural boundaries

Hofstede's cultural dimensions: power distance, individualism-collectivism, masculinity-femininity, uncertainty avoidance, long-term orientation, indulgence-restraint; derived from IBM employee surveys across 50+ countries
Power distance: the degree to which less powerful members accept unequal power distribution; high in most Asian, Latin American, and Arab countries; low in Scandinavia and Israel

Individualism vs. collectivism: U.S., UK, Australia score high on individualism; China, Japan, Latin America score high on collectivism; affects team structures, reward systems, and conflict resolution

Global HR strategy options: ethnocentric (home country practices and people), polycentric (local practices and people), geocentric (best practices and people regardless of nationality), regiocentric (regional consistency)
Staffing international operations: parent country nationals (PCNs), host country nationals (HCNs), third country nationals (TCNs); each has advantages and disadvantages
Expatriate selection: technical competence is necessary but insufficient; cross-cultural adaptability, family situation, and language capability are critical predictors of assignment success
Expatriate failure: early return or poor performance; rates of 20-40% in traditional programs; costs $300K-$1M per failure including repatriation and replacement
Cross-cultural training: before departure and during assignment; covers language, culture, business practices, and family adjustment; research shows it significantly reduces failure rates

Compensation for expatriates: balance sheet approach (maintain home-country standard of living with adjustments for cost, housing, and tax) is common; includes base salary, benefits, allowances, tax equalization
Repatriation: returning expatriates often struggle to find roles that use their international experience; firms lose the investment when repatriates leave within two years (common at 30-50%)
Local labor laws: termination restrictions, mandatory benefits, works councils, collective bargaining requirements vary enormously by country and must be understood before entering a market

Global mobility vs. virtual teams: video conferencing and digital collaboration reduced the need for some expatriate assignments; but building trust and local knowledge still often requires physical presence
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case comparison of two global HR strategies. Walmart in Germany (2006): tried to impose U.S. HR practices including mandatory morning chants, smile policy, ban on workplace relationships between coworkers, and 24/7 store hours. German employees, unions, and works councils rejected these as inappropriate; a court struck down the workplace relationship ban. Combined with operational and real estate missteps, Walmart exited Germany at a loss of over $1 billion in 2006. Contrast with IKEA's global expansion: maintains consistent store layout, product design, and service philosophy, but adapts compensation, benefits, and management practices to local norms. Scandinavian-origin culture of egalitarianism, democratic decision-making, and long-term employee investment travels better to European markets than to some U.S. or Asian markets where it has required local adaptation. Discuss what dimensions can and should be standardized globally (core values, key metrics) vs. localized (compensation, benefits, management style) vs. abandoned in markets where fit is poor.
setup_requirements: Case packet: 'Walmart in Germany' and 'IKEA Global HR'

review.value: Cultural differences in power distance, individualism, and uncertainty tolerance are not stereotypes but measurable patterns that affect what HR practices work where; ignoring them leads to expensive failures

review.key: Expatriate Selection Is About Family Too
review.value: Most expatriate failures are attributed to family issues not job performance; selecting candidates whose family situation supports the assignment is as important as selecting for technical skill
Global firms benefit from consistent culture, values, and key processes; but compensation, benefits, and management style usually need local adaptation; drawing the line requires strategic judgment
The highest ROI on expatriate investment comes from repatriates who bring international experience back to the home organization; firms that lose repatriates waste the investment

class_schedule.topic: Future of HR: AI, Flexible Work, and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Evaluate AI applications in HR and their risks and opportunities
Design policies for flexible, hybrid, and remote work
Synthesize course concepts into a framework for strategic HR decision-making
Prepare for final project and exam
readings: Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 17-18 and updates
Jesuthasan & Boudreau, Reinventing Jobs (2018)
Bloom, 'Hybrid Is the Future of Work,' Stanford SIEPR (2021)
World Economic Forum, Future of Jobs Report (2023)

lecture_notes.introduction: The HR function is being reshaped by two converging forces: the rapid maturation of AI and analytics, and the permanent change in work arrangements following COVID. Generative AI tools can draft job descriptions, screen resumes, conduct initial interviews, personalize learning experiences, answer employee questions, and analyze engagement data at scale. These tools promise efficiency gains and in some cases better outcomes than human processes, but they also risk encoding and amplifying historical biases, reducing human judgment in sensitive decisions, and eroding the employee experience. Amazon famously abandoned an AI resume screener that had learned to penalize women. Vendors now claim AI-driven bias audits and fairness guarantees, but verification is difficult. Meanwhile, remote and hybrid work have permanently changed the employment landscape. Nick Bloom's research at Stanford found that hybrid work arrangements (2-3 days at home, the rest in office) produce similar productivity to fully in-office work with substantially higher retention and satisfaction. But fully remote work shows more mixed results, and concerns about culture, mentorship, and collaboration remain. Managers struggle to evaluate remote workers fairly, and junior employees in particular may miss development opportunities. The HR challenge is to design work arrangements, performance management, and culture-building practices that accommodate flexibility without losing the benefits of coordinated in-person work. This final session synthesizes the course's toolkit and frames the judgment questions that will define HR practice for the next decade.
key_concepts: AI in HR: resume screening, interview analysis (video and audio), chatbots for employee questions, engagement analysis, predictive turnover, personalized learning, performance calibration

AI risks in HR: bias (if training data encodes historical discrimination), opacity (black-box decisions employees cannot understand or challenge), dehumanization (removing human judgment from sensitive decisions)
EEOC guidance on AI: employer liability for AI-driven discrimination; disparate impact analysis still applies; fourth-fifths rule extends to algorithmic selection
Remote work types: fully remote (no office requirement), hybrid (mix of remote and in-office), flexible (employee choice within guidelines), on-site (traditional)

Hybrid work research (Bloom et al.): 2-3 days at home and 2-3 in office produces productivity comparable to full-time in-office with substantially higher satisfaction and retention; fully remote more mixed
Challenges of distributed work: proximity bias (favoring in-office employees for promotion), isolation and mental health effects, blurred work-life boundaries, inequitable collaboration tools
Workforce of the future: the rise of gig and contract work, shorter job tenures, skill obsolescence, lifelong learning expectations
Reskilling and upskilling: World Economic Forum estimates that 50% of workers will need significant reskilling by 2025 due to automation and technology change; firms that fail to reskill lose talent
Gig economy: independent contractors, platform workers (Uber, DoorDash), freelancers; raises questions about benefits, protections, and the social contract
Employee wellbeing: mental health, burnout, and stress have emerged as central HR concerns; investments in EAPs, mental health benefits, and burnout prevention are increasingly standard
HR decision framework: (1) align with strategy; (2) diagnose with data; (3) apply evidence-based practices; (4) respect employees as humans, not just resources; (5) measure outcomes and adjust
lecture_notes.demonstration: Synthesis case: a mid-size tech firm is redesigning its HR system after two years of ad hoc post-COVID changes. Walk through applying all the tools from the course. Strategy clarification: what are the firm's competitive priorities? Selection: is the interview process validated, structured, and bias-tested? Onboarding: is there a realistic job preview and effective first-90-days program? Performance management: has it been simplified to focus on ongoing feedback? Compensation: is pay externally competitive, internally equitable, and transparent? Flexible work: what hybrid policy balances productivity, culture, and equity? DEI: are programs evidence-based or performative? Engagement: what does the data say and what interventions are warranted? Analytics: is HR using data to drive decisions or still relying on intuition? The point is not to memorize tools but to apply them in an integrated way to real problems.

review.value: Every HR practice in this course has limitations and assumptions; using them mechanically produces bad outcomes; good HR judgment knows when a tool applies and when it does not

HR practices shape the experience of real people whose careers, livelihoods, and well-being are affected; practices that treat employees as data points or interchangeable units produce the outcomes they deserve

review.key: Evidence-Based HR Is Hard but Right
review.value: The research literature often contradicts what HR does by habit; firms that align with evidence (structured interviews, transfer-focused training, simple performance systems, voluntary DEI programs) outperform those that do not

The firms with sustained competitive advantage almost universally have distinctive HR practices that attract, develop, and retain distinctive people; HR is not a cost center, it is where competitive advantage lives

quizzes.topic: Strategic HR, Employment Law, and Job Analysis

questions.question: A 200-person technology firm is deciding whether to classify a new role, 'Senior Software Developer,' as exempt or non-exempt under the FLSA. The role pays $95,000 salary, includes code design, architecture, and supervision of one junior developer, and occasionally requires on-call weekend work. (a) Which FLSA exemption(s) might apply, and what are the specific tests? (b) What is the compliance risk if the firm misclassifies the role as exempt when it should be non-exempt? (c) Recommend a defensible classification with reasoning.
questions.answer:
(a) Two exemptions potentially apply:

1. Computer Employee Exemption (FLSA 29 USC 213(a)(17)): Applies to employees paid at least $684/week or $27.63/hour whose primary duties are (i) the application of systems analysis techniques, (ii) the design, development, documentation, analysis, creation, testing, or modification of computer systems or programs, or (iii) design/documentation of machine operating systems. A Senior Software Developer doing design and architecture clearly meets the duties test.

2. Administrative or Executive Exemption: The duties test for executive exemption requires (i) primary duty of management, (ii) customary direction of two or more full-time employees, (iii) authority to hire/fire or strong influence over such decisions. With only one direct report, this role FAILS the executive exemption (requires two FTE-equivalent subordinates).

Salary basis test: At $95,000 salary, the role easily exceeds the $684/week ($35,568/year) minimum salary threshold for white-collar exemptions.

(b) Compliance risk of misclassification:
- Back pay liability: Unpaid overtime for up to 2 years (3 if willful) under FLSA. For a $95K developer working 50 hrs/week average, this is roughly $95,000 x (10 hrs OT at 1.5x)/52 weeks = approximately $27,000/year in unpaid overtime.
- Liquidated (double) damages unless the employer can prove good-faith compliance (29 USC 216).
- Attorney's fees payable to plaintiff (one-way fee shifting under FLSA).
- Individual liability for owners and managers.
- Class/collective action exposure: if one developer sues, similarly-situated employees can opt in.
- State law penalties (California, New York have harsher remedies).

Typical settlements for developer misclassification cases range from $5M to $30M (e.g., IBM class actions, Oracle settlement).

(c) Defensible classification: EXEMPT under the Computer Employee Exemption. The duties test is clearly met (design, development, testing of software) and the salary basis is clearly met ($95,000 >> $35,568 threshold). Document the classification with a job description that explicitly describes the exempt duties, retain the analysis in writing, and review annually. Do not rely on the executive exemption since the role only supervises one employee.

rubric.key: Correct identification of Computer Employee Exemption and its specific duties test
Correct analysis of why Executive Exemption fails (only one direct report)
Accurate description of compliance risks including back pay, liquidated damages, attorney fees
Defensible recommendation with documentation guidance

questions.question: A mid-sized manufacturer receives a resume from a 58-year-old candidate for a supervisor role. The hiring manager comments to HR: 'I'm worried she's overqualified and won't stay long. Let's pass on her.' What laws are implicated, what is the risk, and how should HR respond?
questions.answer:
Laws implicated:

1. Age Discrimination in Employment Act (ADEA) - protects workers age 40+ from employment discrimination. 'Overqualified' can be pretext for age discrimination, particularly when paired with concerns about tenure or energy.

2. State age discrimination laws - many states have broader protections (California FEHA protects all ages; New York protects 18+).

Risk analysis:
- Stereotype-based rejection: 'won't stay long' assumes age predicts turnover, which is empirically false. Research shows older workers often have lower turnover than younger workers.
- 'Overqualified' as pretext: EEOC and courts have repeatedly held that 'overqualified' can mask age bias, especially without objective criteria (EEOC v. Insurance Co. of North America).
- Documentation risk: The manager's comment creates a written record of potentially discriminatory reasoning. Under the McDonnell Douglas framework, this could establish pretext at the prima facie stage of an ADEA claim.
- Disparate impact exposure: if the firm systematically rejects older applicants under vague 'overqualified' rationales, a pattern-or-practice claim becomes possible.

HR response:
1. Immediately intervene: do NOT pass on the candidate based on the manager's reasoning as stated.
2. Coach the manager: explain ADEA protections and that 'overqualified' is not a legally defensible rejection rationale without objective evidence.
3. Redirect the evaluation: require assessment against job-related criteria (specific skills, experience, demonstrated performance) and structured interviews.
4. Document the coaching: create a contemporaneous note showing HR caught and corrected the bias.
5. If concerns about fit remain after objective evaluation, document job-related reasons in writing.
6. Review broader patterns: pull aggregate hiring data by age group for the past 12 months to detect systemic issues (adverse impact analysis).
7. Training: schedule bias-awareness training for hiring managers if recurrence is a concern.

rubric.key: Correct identification of ADEA and age discrimination risk
Explanation of 'overqualified' and tenure concerns as potential pretext
HR intervention with manager coaching and redirection to job-related criteria
Documentation and systemic response (aggregate analysis, training)

questions.question: Explain the difference between task-oriented job analysis and worker-oriented (competency-based) job analysis. Give one specific example where each approach is appropriate, and describe how the output would look for a Customer Service Representative role using each method.
questions.answer:
Task-oriented job analysis focuses on WHAT gets done: the discrete tasks, duties, responsibilities, and work activities performed on the job. Output: task statements (action verb + object + purpose), frequency, importance, and required tools/equipment. Most appropriate for roles with clearly defined, repeatable procedures where compliance with specific steps matters: manufacturing operators, pharmacy technicians, air traffic controllers, regulated financial processing roles. Also essential for FLSA classification analysis and ADA reasonable-accommodation decisions that require identification of essential functions.

Worker-oriented (competency-based) job analysis focuses on WHO can do it well: the knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics (KSAOs) required of the person performing the job. Output: competency models with behavioral anchors, KSAO lists, and often linked to 360-degree feedback and career development. Most appropriate for roles where success depends on how work is done rather than what is done: consultants, managers, sales representatives, knowledge workers. Also used when designing training curricula and performance management systems.

Customer Service Representative - task-oriented output:
- Answer inbound calls using ACD system within 3 rings
- Authenticate caller identity using account number and security question
- Retrieve customer account in CRM (Salesforce Service Cloud)
- Document call reason using standard category codes
- Process refund requests up to $500 without escalation
- Transfer escalated issues to Tier 2 using warm-transfer protocol
- Complete post-call survey invitation
Measurement: average handle time, calls per hour, first-call resolution rate.

Customer Service Representative - worker-oriented (competency) output:
- Active listening (ability to hear and reflect customer concerns accurately)
- Emotional regulation (ability to remain calm with angry customers)
- Empathy (ability to demonstrate understanding of customer frustration)
- Problem-solving (ability to diagnose root cause and identify solutions)
- Product knowledge (depth of knowledge about company products/services)
- Written communication (ability to compose clear follow-up emails)
- Adaptability (ability to adjust approach based on customer type)
Measurement: customer satisfaction scores, quality monitoring ratings, 360 feedback from peers and supervisors.

In practice, most sophisticated job analysis combines both: task analysis for legal/compliance purposes and competency modeling for development/selection.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between task-oriented (what) and worker-oriented (who/how) approaches
Appropriate example contexts for each approach with reasoning
Specific, differentiated outputs for CSR under each method

questions.question: A company is choosing between two selection tests for a sales position. Test A is a cognitive ability test with validity (r) of 0.51 and costs $15 per candidate. Test B is a personality assessment with validity (r) of 0.22 and costs $35 per candidate. The firm hires 50 sales reps per year from 500 applicants, and the standard deviation of job performance (SDy) is estimated at $25,000. Using the Schmidt-Hunter utility formula, (a) calculate the expected dollar value of using each test versus no test, and (b) recommend which test to use with reasoning.
questions.answer:
Schmidt-Hunter utility formula (per hire):
Utility = r * SDy * Z
where r = validity coefficient, SDy = standard deviation of job performance in dollars, Z = average standard score of selected applicants (depends on selection ratio).

Selection ratio (SR) = 50/500 = 0.10
For SR = 0.10, Z (average z-score of selectees from a normal distribution) is approximately 1.76.

Total utility per hire = r * SDy * Z - cost_per_candidate * (applicants/hires)
Annual utility = per-hire utility * number of hires

Test A (Cognitive ability, r = 0.51):
Utility per hire (gross) = 0.51 * $25,000 * 1.76 = $22,440
Cost per hire = $15 * 500/50 = $150 testing cost per hire
Net utility per hire = $22,440 - $150 = $22,290
Annual utility = $22,290 * 50 = $1,114,500

Test B (Personality, r = 0.22):
Utility per hire (gross) = 0.22 * $25,000 * 1.76 = $9,680
Cost per hire = $35 * 500/50 = $350 testing cost per hire
Net utility per hire = $9,680 - $350 = $9,330
Annual utility = $9,330 * 50 = $466,500

Difference: Test A generates $648,000 more value per year than Test B.

(b) Recommendation: Use Test A (cognitive ability).
Reasoning:
- Test A delivers 2.4x the utility of Test B.
- Despite Test B's lower per-candidate cost, the testing cost is negligible relative to the performance-value differential: the validity (0.51 vs 0.22) dominates the cost.
- Cognitive ability is the single most valid predictor of job performance across nearly all jobs (Schmidt & Hunter, 1998 meta-analysis), especially for knowledge-intensive work like sales.
- Consideration: Pair Test A with structured interviews (incremental validity) and a work sample if feasible. Do NOT pair Test A with Test B standalone since personality tests add only modest incremental validity and carry higher adverse impact concerns when used alone.
- Adverse impact check: Cognitive ability tests have known subgroup differences (roughly 1 SD between White and Black applicants on general cognitive ability). Monitor 4/5ths rule compliance; consider banding or top-down within band approaches if adverse impact emerges.

rubric.key: Correct application of Schmidt-Hunter utility formula with SR, Z, r, SDy
Correct calculation of net utility per hire and annual utility for both tests
Defensible recommendation citing validity dominance and adverse impact consideration

questions.question: A manufacturing company has the following hiring data for the past year: 100 white applicants, 50 hired (50% hire rate); 80 Black applicants, 20 hired (25% hire rate); 40 Hispanic applicants, 16 hired (40% hire rate). (a) Apply the 4/5ths (80%) rule to identify any adverse impact. (b) What does adverse impact trigger legally under Title VII, and what defense is available to the employer?
questions.answer:
(a) 4/5ths (80%) Rule Analysis:

Hire rates by group:
- White: 50/100 = 50.0% (highest rate, use as baseline)
- Hispanic: 16/40 = 40.0%
- Black: 20/80 = 25.0%

4/5ths threshold: 4/5 * 50.0% = 40.0%

Comparison:
- Hispanic ratio: 40.0% / 50.0% = 80.0% - AT the threshold, technically not adverse impact (borderline)
- Black ratio: 25.0% / 50.0% = 50.0% - BELOW 80%, ADVERSE IMPACT indicated

(b) Legal implications under Title VII:

Adverse impact triggers a prima facie case of disparate impact discrimination under Title VII (Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 1971, codified in Civil Rights Act 1991 Section 703(k)).

Once adverse impact is established, the burden shifts to the employer:
1. Employer defense: The employer must show that the challenged selection practice is 'job-related for the position in question and consistent with business necessity.' This typically requires showing:
   - Validation evidence meeting Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures (1978) standards: criterion-related, content, or construct validity.
   - Evidence that the test or procedure measures KSAs actually required for the job.

2. Plaintiff rebuttal: Even if the employer establishes business necessity, plaintiffs can still prevail by showing an alternative employment practice exists that (a) serves the employer's interests and (b) has less discriminatory impact, which the employer refused to adopt.

Concrete actions the employer should take:
- Conduct a job analysis and validation study for the selection procedures causing adverse impact.
- Review each step of the selection process (resume screening, tests, interviews) for specific sources of disparity.
- Explore alternative assessments with lower adverse impact (e.g., work samples, structured interviews, biodata have less impact than cognitive tests).
- Document the 4/5ths analysis and response plan in writing.
- Consider OFCCP audit exposure if the employer is a federal contractor (additional compliance obligations).

rubric.key: Correct hire rate calculations and identification of White as reference group
Correct 4/5ths rule application identifying adverse impact on Black applicants
Correct explanation of disparate impact legal framework under Title VII
Business necessity defense and plaintiff rebuttal steps correctly described

questions.question: Explain why structured interviews have roughly twice the predictive validity (r ≈ 0.51) of unstructured interviews (r ≈ 0.20), citing at least three specific structural elements that contribute to the difference.
questions.answer:
Structured interviews consistently outperform unstructured interviews because they control multiple sources of measurement error and bias. Schmidt & Hunter's 1998 meta-analysis found structured interviews have validity approaching cognitive ability tests, while unstructured interviews hover near resume review in predictive value.

Structural elements that drive the validity gap:

1. Standardized, job-relevant questions derived from job analysis. Structured interviews use the same questions for every candidate, drawn from an explicit job analysis identifying critical KSAOs. Unstructured interviews wander based on candidate resume, rapport, or interviewer interests, measuring different things for different candidates. Standardization makes apples-to-apples comparison possible and forces the interviewer to focus on job-relevant dimensions.

2. Behavioral or situational question format. Structured interviews use behavioral questions ('Tell me about a time when you handled a difficult customer complaint. What was the situation, what did you do, and what was the outcome?') or situational questions ('Imagine a customer demands a refund you cannot legally provide. How would you respond?'). Both formats probe specific job-relevant behaviors. Unstructured 'getting to know you' conversations rarely elicit performance-relevant data.

3. Anchored rating scales (BARS - Behaviorally Anchored Rating Scales). Each question is scored on a 1-5 scale with behavioral anchors describing what a 1, 3, or 5 response looks like. This reduces leniency, severity, and halo errors, and enables inter-rater reliability. Unstructured interviews typically produce global impressions that are unreliable across raters.

4. Multiple interviewers with independent scoring, averaged at the end. Structured panels score independently and then discuss. This averages out individual bias and idiosyncratic judgments. Unstructured single-interviewer decisions are prone to first-impression bias, similar-to-me bias, and confirmatory questioning.

5. Reduced reliance on irrelevant information. Structured interviews prohibit questions about hobbies, family, age, and other information that can trigger implicit bias. Unstructured interviews frequently collect irrelevant or protected-class information that introduces noise and legal risk.

6. Note-taking requirements and documentation. Structured interviews require contemporaneous notes tied to each question, creating an audit trail and forcing active listening rather than rapport-building.

The result: structured interviews approach cognitive ability tests in validity (r ≈ 0.51 vs r ≈ 0.54) while providing face validity and legal defensibility that pure cognitive tests lack.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies three or more structural elements with job-analysis foundation
Explains how each element reduces bias or improves measurement quality
References empirical validity figures and connects to job-relevance/legal defensibility

questions.question: Apply Kirkpatrick's four-level training evaluation model to a new sales training program. For each level, specify (a) what is measured, (b) a concrete measurement method, and (c) a typical challenge.
questions.answer:
Kirkpatrick's Four Levels of Training Evaluation (1959, updated 2006 with Level 5 ROI by Phillips):

Level 1 - Reaction
(a) What is measured: Participant satisfaction and perceived relevance of the training ('smile sheets').
(b) Measurement method: Post-training survey with Likert-scale items (course quality, instructor effectiveness, content relevance, intent to apply). Some firms add net promoter score.
(c) Typical challenge: Reaction data has near-zero correlation with actual learning or behavior change. High reaction scores can mislead management into believing the program is effective when no learning occurred.

Level 2 - Learning
(a) What is measured: Knowledge, skill, and attitude change from the training.
(b) Measurement method: Pre-test and post-test of content knowledge (multiple choice), skill demonstration (role-play of sales conversation), or simulation performance. Control group ideal for causal attribution.
(c) Typical challenge: Learning can decay quickly (Ebbinghaus forgetting curve). Test validity depends on good item design - superficial item writing produces invalid measurement.

Level 3 - Behavior
(a) What is measured: Transfer of learning to the workplace - are participants actually using what they learned?
(b) Measurement method: Supervisor observation and coaching feedback, 360-degree feedback at 3 and 6 months post-training, ride-alongs with sales reps to observe application of training skills, self-report with triangulation. Behavioral change checklist against training content.
(c) Typical challenge: Transfer is notoriously low without supportive environment. Without supervisor reinforcement, manager coaching, and opportunities to practice, behavior reverts. The 'transfer problem' requires organizational supports beyond the training event itself.

Level 4 - Results
(a) What is measured: Business outcomes attributable to the training (for sales: revenue per rep, close rate, sales cycle length, customer retention).
(b) Measurement method: Compare trained vs. control group metrics before and after, longitudinal tracking, controlling for seasonality and other confounders. For a sales program: did close rate improve for trained reps relative to baseline and untrained peers?
(c) Typical challenge: Attribution is hard. Sales results are influenced by economy, product changes, pricing, competition, manager quality, and individual differences. Isolating the training effect requires rigorous design (control groups, statistical controls, time-series analysis).

Level 5 (Phillips ROI, optional):
Calculate ROI = (monetary benefits - cost of training) / cost of training. Benefits quantified from Level 4 outcomes (e.g., $X additional revenue per trained rep).

Best practice: Evaluate at Level 3 or higher for any significant training investment. Level 1 alone is insufficient to justify program continuation or improvement decisions.

rubric.key: Correct identification and definition of all four Kirkpatrick levels
Specific, appropriate measurement methods for each level
Thoughtful challenges for each level including transfer problem and attribution difficulty

questions.question: A software engineering manager is preparing annual performance reviews for her team of 10. She is using a forced distribution (stack ranking) system requiring 20% top performers, 70% middle, and 10% bottom. Identify three problems with forced distribution in this context and recommend a better alternative.
questions.answer:
Three problems with forced distribution in this context:

1. Statistical nonsense at small team sizes. With only 10 engineers, the 20/70/10 distribution forces exactly 2 top, 7 middle, and 1 bottom. But performance on a 10-person team does not naturally follow this distribution - especially if the team is a high-performing group hired through rigorous selection. The manager is forced to rank a fully competent engineer as 'bottom' simply to satisfy the quota. Performance distributions in knowledge work are often Pareto (a few stars, most solid, few weak) but also frequently have no weak performers at all on well-managed teams. Forced distribution creates artificial underperformers.

2. Damages collaboration and team dynamics. Software engineering is fundamentally collaborative - code reviews, pair programming, knowledge sharing, shared systems. Forced ranking creates zero-sum competition where helping a peer improve harms the helper's relative position. Microsoft's 'stack ranking' era (ended 2013) is a case study: engineers hoarded information, avoided working with strong teammates (to avoid being outshone), and focused on narrow individual metrics. Steven Sinofsky and others have documented how stack ranking contributed to Microsoft's 'lost decade' of innovation.

3. Legal and DEI risk. Forced distribution systems have been the basis of multiple discrimination lawsuits (Ford Motor Company, Goodyear, Microsoft). Statistical analyses often reveal that women, older workers, and minorities cluster in the 'bottom' buckets at disproportionate rates, creating disparate impact liability. Without explicit bias-mitigation protocols, forced ranking amplifies manager bias by requiring low ratings.

Additional problems:
- Subverts manager coaching: the manager must rank and fire good performers instead of coaching them.
- Turnover of top performers: those ranked 'middle' in a strong group often leave for competitors where they would be 'top.'
- Rater bias: managers shift their criteria to fit the quota rather than applying consistent standards.

Recommended alternative: Calibration-based absolute rating system with regular feedback.

How it works:
- Managers rate each engineer against absolute standards for their level (e.g., 'meets expectations for Senior Engineer II') based on specific competencies: technical depth, code quality, system design, collaboration, business impact.
- Calibration meetings: managers across the org meet to discuss ratings and ensure consistent application of standards across teams (anti-inflation, anti-halo).
- Continuous feedback: regular 1:1s, quarterly check-ins, and real-time feedback replace the single annual review event.
- Performance improvement plans for genuine underperformance, applied individually with clear expectations and support, not as a quota.
- Pay differentiation from absolute ratings: strong performers get larger raises and promotions; weak performers get PIPs or managed out.

Examples: Google, Adobe (replaced annual reviews with 'Check-In'), Deloitte (eliminated annual ratings in 2015), GE (replaced Jack Welch's forced ranking with the 'PD@GE' app).

rubric.key: Three substantive problems including small-sample statistics, collaboration damage, and legal/DEI risk
Specific real-world examples (Microsoft, Ford, etc.)
Concrete alternative with calibration, absolute ratings, and continuous feedback

questions.question: Design a BARS (Behaviorally Anchored Rating Scale) for the dimension 'Customer Service Quality' for a customer service representative role. Provide behavioral anchors at levels 1 (poor), 3 (satisfactory), and 5 (outstanding).
questions.answer:
BARS Dimension: Customer Service Quality
Definition: Extent to which the CSR interacts with customers in a manner that resolves issues, builds loyalty, and represents the brand positively.

Level 5 - OUTSTANDING:
Anchor: 'Proactively greets the customer by name, listens without interrupting, accurately paraphrases the customer's concern, offers a creative solution that exceeds policy minimums where appropriate, verifies customer satisfaction with the resolution, and concludes with a personalized, brand-consistent closing. Example: When a customer calls about a delayed shipment, the CSR apologizes sincerely, expedites a replacement at no charge, offers a goodwill credit, confirms the new delivery date, and follows up with a personal email.'

Level 4:
Anchor: 'Greets the customer warmly, listens actively, correctly identifies the issue, provides an accurate solution within policy, and confirms resolution before ending the call. Example: Responds to a billing inquiry by reviewing the account, explaining the charges clearly, processing an adjustment per policy, and confirming the customer understands.'

Level 3 - SATISFACTORY:
Anchor: 'Greets the customer politely, listens to the issue, identifies the correct solution path, and processes the resolution accurately using standard procedures. Tone is professional but not memorably positive. Example: Handles a return request by verifying eligibility, generating the RMA number, and providing shipping instructions in a routine transaction.'

Level 2:
Anchor: 'Greets the customer with a scripted opening, sometimes misses the customer's underlying concern, requires prompting to find the correct solution, and provides a mechanically correct but impersonal response. Example: Answers a question about product features by reading from the knowledge base without adapting to the customer's specific use case, requiring the customer to repeat themselves.'

Level 1 - POOR:
Anchor: 'Uses curt or dismissive tone, interrupts the customer, fails to accurately diagnose the issue before attempting a solution, cites policy without offering alternatives, and ends the call without confirming resolution. Example: Tells a frustrated customer 'that's our policy' without explaining options, places the customer on hold for 10 minutes without checking back, and hangs up without confirming satisfaction.'

Notes for use:
- Anchors were developed through critical incident workshops with CSR supervisors, agents, and customer survey feedback.
- Rating is based on observed behavior during live call monitoring (random sample of 5 calls per month per agent) and supplemented by customer survey scores.
- Anchors must be re-validated annually to reflect changing products, policies, and customer expectations.

rubric.key: Clear dimension definition linked to job performance
Behavioral (not trait) anchors at levels 1, 3, and 5 as specified
Anchors differentiate meaningfully between levels with concrete examples
Practical notes on development and use (critical incidents, monitoring)

questions.question: Compare Costco's and Walmart's (Sam's Club) compensation strategies using the concept of efficiency wages and total compensation cost. Costco pays its warehouse workers an average of $26/hour with full benefits; Sam's Club historically paid around $15/hour with more limited benefits. Yet Costco reports lower cost per sale and higher profitability per employee. How does this work economically?
questions.answer:
This apparent paradox is resolved by analyzing TOTAL labor cost per unit of output, not wage rate alone. The efficiency wage theory (Akerlof, Yellen, Shapiro-Stiglitz) explains how above-market wages can be economically rational when they generate productivity and retention benefits that exceed their direct cost.

Mechanisms by which Costco's higher wages reduce total cost:

1. Turnover reduction. Costco's turnover is roughly 6% annually vs. retail industry average 60%+. Each warehouse employee replacement costs approximately $3,000-$7,000 (recruiting, training, productivity ramp, supervisor time). At 100 employees per warehouse, Costco saves roughly (54% * 100 * $5,000) = $270,000 per warehouse per year in turnover cost compared to the industry average.

2. Productivity per employee. Costco's sales per employee is approximately $600,000 vs. Walmart's $225,000. Part of this is the warehouse club model, but significant differentiation comes from tenured, engaged workers: faster scanning, better inventory handling, superior customer service that drives membership renewals (Costco's 90%+ renewal rate depends on employee-customer interactions).

3. Reduced training cost. Veteran employees require less training and supervision. Costco's training investment is concentrated on long-tenured employees who deliver return on the investment over many years.

4. Reduced shrinkage and errors. Engaged, well-paid employees have lower theft rates, fewer cash handling errors, and better loss prevention. Costco reports shrink rates 1/3 of industry average.

5. Culture and recruitment self-selection. Costco's reputation as a good employer attracts stronger applicant pools - more applicants per opening, better screening, better-fit hires. The selection ratio improves utility (per Schmidt-Hunter).

6. Lower healthcare costs through coverage scale. Costco covers nearly all hourly workers; this creates actuarial scale, lowers per-employee premiums, reduces presenteeism, and lowers long-term healthcare cost.

7. Avoids unionization, regulatory scrutiny, and reputational cost. Costco has faced almost no major labor disputes. Walmart has faced continuous labor litigation, OSHA complaints, and reputational damage (some estimated at 1-2% of sales).

Total compensation cost math (illustrative):
Costco: $26/hr * 2,080 hrs + 25% benefits = $67,600/year loaded. With 6% turnover, effective cost = $67,600 * 1.03 (training amortization) = ~$69,600.
Walmart: $15/hr * 2,080 hrs + 15% benefits = $35,880/year loaded. With 60% turnover, effective cost = $35,880 * 1.30 (turnover and training) = ~$46,600.
Direct labor cost per hour: Costco is 49% higher.
But productivity per employee: Costco is 167% higher.
Result: Labor cost per dollar of sales is LOWER at Costco.

The strategic lesson: Wage rate is the wrong metric for labor cost. Total cost per unit of output, including turnover, productivity, quality, and ancillary costs, is the correct measure. Efficiency wage strategies work when the productivity and retention benefits exceed the wage premium. They fail when the firm cannot or does not capture the productivity benefit (e.g., jobs where individual effort does not affect output).

rubric.key: Correct framing in terms of total labor cost per unit of output, not wage rate
Three or more specific mechanisms (turnover, productivity, training, shrinkage, culture)
Quantitative illustration showing how the math works despite higher wage rate

questions.question: A non-union company has heard rumors that the UAW is trying to organize its workforce. The CEO asks HR: 'What can we legally do to discourage unionization without violating the NLRA?' List three legal strategies and three things the company CANNOT legally do.
questions.answer:
Legal framework: The National Labor Relations Act (NLRA) Section 7 protects employees' right to self-organization. Section 8(a) identifies unfair labor practices by employers. The framework around legal vs. illegal employer responses is governed by the 'TIPS' rule - employers cannot Threaten, Interrogate, make Promises, or engage in Surveillance.

Three LEGAL employer strategies:

1. Share FACTS about unionization. The employer may provide accurate information: dues costs, strike history, collective bargaining impacts on flexibility, specific outcomes at similar facilities. The employer may explain how wages and benefits are determined at the company and in other unionized and non-unionized firms. Information must be factually accurate.

2. Share OPINIONS about the company's position. The employer may state that it believes employees are better off without a union, that the company prefers to work directly with employees. First Amendment and NLRA Section 8(c) protect non-coercive speech expressing the employer's views.

3. Improve the conditions that drive organizing BEFORE any union activity. Most unionization is driven by unresolved employee concerns: arbitrary supervision, stagnant wages, unsafe conditions, disrespectful treatment, poor benefits. The employer may invest in improving management quality, wages, benefits, communication, and grievance mechanisms - provided these changes are not timed in response to a specific campaign. Once organizing is known, you cannot make new benefit promises.

Additional legal strategies:
- Hold legally compliant informational meetings (captive audience meetings are currently legal but under regulatory review by the Biden-era NLRB).
- Enforce existing work rules (solicitation, distribution) in a non-discriminatory way and consistently.
- Train supervisors on TIPS rules and what they can/cannot say.
- Conduct legitimate employee opinion surveys if done before union activity is known.

Three ILLEGAL responses (unfair labor practices under NLRA Section 8(a)):

1. THREATS (Section 8(a)(1)). The employer cannot threaten adverse consequences for supporting the union: plant closure, job loss, wage reduction, loss of benefits, or termination. This includes both direct threats and implied threats ('it would be a shame if we had to close this plant'). Threatening to move work, outsource, or automate in response to union activity is a textbook ULP.

2. INTERROGATION (Section 8(a)(1)). The employer cannot ask employees about their union sympathies, who signed authorization cards, or what happens at union meetings. Even casual supervisor questions ('So what do you think about this union stuff?') can be coercive.

3. PROMISES (Section 8(a)(1)). The employer cannot promise new benefits, wage increases, or favorable treatment in exchange for rejecting the union. A sudden raise announced during the campaign period is highly suspect and typically becomes a ULP.

4. SURVEILLANCE (Section 8(a)(1)). The employer cannot monitor union meetings, organizing activities, or which employees are involved. Video surveillance of union activities, stationing supervisors at meeting locations, or monitoring employee communications about the union are all ULPs.

5. DISCRIMINATION (Section 8(a)(3)). Cannot discipline, demote, or terminate employees for union activity. Any adverse action against a union supporter must be supported by compelling pre-existing evidence. Amazon's firing of Chris Smalls in 2020 is being litigated as a Section 8(a)(3) case.

6. REFUSAL TO BARGAIN (Section 8(a)(5)). Once a union is certified, the employer must bargain in good faith. Refusing to meet, stalling, or unilateral changes to wages/conditions without bargaining are ULPs.

Consequences of ULPs: NLRB can order reinstatement with back pay, posting of notices admitting the violation, bargaining orders (in extreme cases where the union would have won absent ULPs - Gissel bargaining orders), and damage to brand reputation.

rubric.key: Three legitimate employer strategies correctly identified with NLRA compliance
Three specific ULP categories identified under TIPS framework
Specific legal citations or examples (Section 8(a), Gissel, Amazon case)

questions.question: Explain the difference between pay equity (internal alignment) and pay competitiveness (external alignment), and describe how a compensation manager uses job evaluation and salary surveys to achieve both.
questions.answer:
Pay equity (internal alignment) refers to the fairness of pay relationships AMONG jobs within an organization. Employees compare their pay to peers and predecessors in the same firm. Perceived internal inequity (a peer making more for similar work) is a primary driver of turnover, grievances, and reduced motivation (Adams' equity theory). Internal alignment is achieved through JOB EVALUATION: systematic assessment of the relative worth of jobs based on compensable factors.

Job evaluation methods:
- Point-factor method (most common): jobs are rated on compensable factors like knowledge, skill, responsibility, effort, working conditions. Each factor is weighted and scored; total points determine the job's place in the internal hierarchy and its pay grade. Hay Group Point-Factor is the most widely used proprietary system.
- Ranking: simple ordering of jobs from most to least valuable. Works for small organizations but not scalable.
- Classification: jobs are grouped into pre-defined grades with generic descriptions (e.g., GS levels in federal government).
- Market pricing: use market data to determine the pay grade (hybrid with external alignment).

Pay competitiveness (external alignment) refers to the firm's pay relative to the LABOR MARKET - other employers who hire similar talent. This is the relevant comparison for attraction (getting candidates to accept offers) and external turnover (losing employees to competitors). External alignment is achieved through SALARY SURVEYS.

Salary survey process:
1. Identify relevant market: competitors for talent (not just industry peers, but employers with similar jobs in the same geography).
2. Select benchmark jobs: 40-60% of jobs that can be matched to standard market descriptions (cashier, software engineer II, VP Marketing).
3. Subscribe to surveys: Mercer, Aon Radford, Willis Towers Watson, Culpepper (tech), industry-specific surveys. Costs typically $5,000-$50,000 per survey.
4. Participate in surveys as a condition of receiving competitor data.
5. Age the data to a common effective date (e.g., all to January 1).
6. Calculate market percentiles (25th, 50th, 75th) and decide on pay positioning: below market (cost leader, e.g., Walmart), at market (follower), above market (premium payer, e.g., Google pays 75th percentile).

Combining both: the compensation manager builds a pay structure with grades from job evaluation (internal equity) and pay ranges (minimum, midpoint, maximum) set based on survey data (external competitiveness). The midpoint of each grade is typically set at the firm's target market percentile. Employees move through the range based on performance and tenure.

Example:
- Job evaluation ranks Software Engineer II as Grade 7.
- Salary survey shows Software Engineer II market median is $120,000.
- The company pays at 60th percentile, so midpoint is $125,000.
- Range is midpoint +/- 20%: $100,000 to $150,000.
- New hires start near the minimum; high performers progress to the maximum; further growth requires promotion to Grade 8 (Senior Software Engineer).

Tensions between internal and external alignment arise when market pay for certain jobs (e.g., data scientists, specialty surgeons) outpaces internal grade structures. Resolutions include market supplements, re-grading, dual career ladders (technical track paid competitively without management hierarchy), and targeted retention bonuses.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between internal and external alignment with behavioral consequences
Accurate description of job evaluation methods (point-factor, etc.)
Accurate description of salary survey process and pay structure construction
Discussion of tensions and resolutions between internal and external alignment

quizzes.topic: HR Analytics, DEI, and International HRM

questions.question: A retail company's HR analytics team built a logistic regression model to predict 90-day turnover from new hires. The model found these significant predictors: (a) commute distance (positive), (b) starting wage relative to market (negative), (c) referral source (referred employees have lower turnover), (d) supervisor tenure (negative), (e) age (negative - older employees have lower turnover). How should the company USE and NOT USE this model?
questions.answer:
USE the model appropriately:

1. Root-cause diagnosis and policy redesign. The coefficients identify the drivers of 90-day turnover. The company should address the modifiable factors:
- Commute distance: consider location strategy for new stores, hiring zone preferences, transportation subsidies, or flexible scheduling to make long commutes manageable.
- Wage competitiveness: raise starting wages to market median or above - the model quantifies the ROI (turnover cost avoided) of the wage increase.
- Supervisor quality (proxied by tenure): invest in supervisor selection and training. High turnover under specific supervisors is a coaching opportunity.
- Referral strategy: expand referral bonuses and systematize referral solicitation since referred employees stay longer.

2. Realistic Job Previews and expectation setting. Use the commute finding to give candidates realistic information about the commute demands of each location before offer acceptance, so self-selection filters out candidates who will regret long commutes.

3. Onboarding risk stratification. Flag new hires with multiple risk factors (long commute + below-market wage + new supervisor) for additional onboarding support, early check-ins, and intervention.

4. Forecasting and workforce planning. Use the model to predict near-term staffing needs and budget for replacement hiring.

DO NOT USE the model for:

1. SELECTION based on protected characteristics. Age is a protected characteristic under ADEA. Using age as a selection factor ('hire older workers because they stay longer') is direct age discrimination and illegal - even if age is statistically predictive. The same applies to any model using race, sex, disability, national origin, religion, or other protected class as a selection criterion.

2. SELECTION based on proxies for protected characteristics. Commute distance can be a proxy for neighborhood, which can proxy for race (due to residential segregation). Using commute distance as a selection filter can create disparate impact liability. Referral source can replicate existing workforce demographics (homogeneous networks), violating diversity goals and potentially creating disparate impact if the existing workforce is non-representative.

3. Individual adverse action without procedural protections. Do not fire or withhold promotion based on the model's prediction that an individual WILL turnover - this is both unfair and legally risky. Models predict probabilities, not individual outcomes.

4. Hiding the model from workers. Algorithmic management raises trust and transparency concerns. Workers should understand the factors that drive company decisions affecting them, especially where those factors are modifiable.

5. Assuming the model will remain valid without monitoring. Models drift as labor markets, compensation, and the workforce change. The team should re-validate annually and retire or retrain the model if coefficients shift.

Key principle: Analytics should inform organizational policy (what can we change about the system?), not individual adverse actions (who do we reject or fire?). Treat the model as a diagnostic of systemic issues rather than a scoring tool for individual selection.

Legal citation: Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures (EEOC) require validation and adverse impact analysis before using any predictive model in hiring decisions. EEOC's 2023 AI guidance explicitly addresses algorithmic hiring and disparate impact.

rubric.key: Legitimate uses focused on policy and system-level interventions
Correct identification of age as illegal selection criterion under ADEA
Discussion of proxy variable risk (commute, referrals as diversity concerns)
Principle distinction between policy use and individual adverse action

questions.question: A US multinational is expatriating an American manager to run its new subsidiary in Tokyo for three years. Identify four key HR decisions the company must make for this assignment, using international HRM frameworks.
questions.answer:
Four key HR decisions for the Tokyo assignment:

1. Staffing Approach: Ethnocentric, Polycentric, Geocentric, or Regiocentric?
Perlmutter's framework describes four staffing approaches:
- Ethnocentric: key positions filled by parent-country nationals (PCNs) - the American manager is PCN.
- Polycentric: host-country nationals (HCNs) fill subsidiary management.
- Geocentric: best person regardless of nationality.
- Regiocentric: best person within a region.

The company has chosen an ethnocentric approach for this role. Rationale (typical): bring US-specific expertise, ensure strategic alignment with HQ, develop the manager for future global roles. Alternative considerations: HCN would have language, cultural, and relationship advantages; PCN may struggle with Japanese business norms (keiretsu relationships, consensus decision-making, amae, wa).

Recommendation: Use PCN for first year with mandatory knowledge transfer to HCN successor by end of year 3, or pair PCN with strong Japanese #2 from day one.

2. Compensation Method: Balance Sheet, Going Rate, or Hybrid?
The most common method for expatriates is the Balance Sheet Approach, which maintains the expatriate's home-country purchasing power plus incentives:
- Home-country base salary (e.g., $180,000 US).
- Cost-of-living allowance (COLA): Tokyo cost-of-living index vs. home city; typically calculated using Mercer or ECA International indexes. Tokyo index relative to New York is roughly 110-120.
- Housing allowance: Tokyo housing is extraordinarily expensive for expat-quality housing; typically $5,000-$12,000/month.
- Hardship/mobility premium: Japan is low hardship but typically 10-15% mobility premium for expatriation.
- Tax equalization: the company ensures the manager pays no more or less total tax than they would in the US (hypothetical tax subtracted from US salary; company pays actual Japanese and US taxes).
- Total package may be 2-3x the home salary.

3. Pre-departure and Cultural Preparation.
Research (Black & Mendenhall, Tung) shows expatriate failure rates of 16-40% driven primarily by cultural adjustment failures, not technical competence. Required preparation:
- Cross-cultural training: Japanese business culture, communication styles (indirect, high-context), decision-making (ringi-sho, nemawashi), relationship-building patterns.
- Language training: even for English-speaking business environments, basic Japanese dramatically aids relationships and daily life.
- Family preparation: spouse and dependents receive their own cultural training; spouse employment is a major adjustment issue in Japan due to work visa restrictions and social norms.
- Housing and schools reconnaissance: pre-departure trip to identify housing and international schools for dependents.
- Mentoring: connect with an experienced Japan expatriate from the firm or executive coach.

4. Performance Management and Repatriation Planning.
Expatriate performance management must account for:
- Different cultural context: Japanese business timelines are longer, relationships develop slowly, 'quick wins' expected in US may be impossible.
- Dual reporting: the expatriate reports to both the Tokyo GM role and the US parent; conflicting expectations must be reconciled.
- Rating criteria: use absolute standards, not relative ratings, and include host-country leader input.

Repatriation planning is critical and often neglected - Tung and Black research shows ~25% of repatriated managers leave the firm within one year of return because no role was prepared for them. Best practices:
- Career path planning BEFORE departure: what role will the manager return to in 3 years?
- Mentor at HQ to maintain visibility during assignment.
- Home leave schedule (typically 1 trip per year funded).
- Knowledge capture: how will the Japan experience be used when the manager returns?
- Financial repatriation support: tax filings, relocation, spouse re-employment.

Additional decisions (bonus): visa and immigration (Status of Residence, work permit), healthcare (international insurance), security and emergency planning, exit protocol if assignment ends early.

rubric.key: Perlmutter's staffing framework correctly applied to PCN/HCN decision
Balance Sheet compensation approach with specific components (COLA, housing, tax equalization)
Pre-departure preparation including cross-cultural training and failure prevention
Repatriation planning addressing known 25% post-return turnover

questions.question: A large company is designing a new DEI initiative. What is the difference between 'quotas' (illegal), 'goals and timetables' (legal for federal contractors), and 'voluntary affirmative action' (legal under specific conditions)? Cite the relevant legal framework.
questions.answer:
The legal framework distinguishes sharply between quotas, goals, and voluntary affirmative action, with the line drawn by the Supreme Court in Johnson v. Transportation Agency (1987) and refined by subsequent cases including Ricci v. DeStefano (2009) and Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard (2023).

1. QUOTAS - ILLEGAL except in rare court-ordered remedies.
Definition: A quota is a FIXED numerical allocation based on a protected characteristic - 'we will hire 20 Black candidates this year regardless of the applicant pool' or 'half of promotions must go to women.' Quotas reserve specific slots based on protected class.

Legal status:
- Illegal under Title VII except as court-ordered remedy for proven, persistent, intentional discrimination (Firefighters v. Stotts, 1984).
- Title VII Section 703(j) explicitly states that nothing in the Act requires preferential treatment based on imbalance.
- SFFA v. Harvard (2023) ended race-conscious admissions in higher education, tightening limits on any protected-class-based selection.

2. GOALS AND TIMETABLES - LEGAL for Federal Contractors, Required by Executive Order 11246.
Definition: Goals are TARGETS for increasing representation of protected groups, accompanied by timetables for achieving them and good-faith efforts. Goals are NOT quotas because they do not reserve slots and are not mandatory achievements.

Legal framework:
- Required for federal contractors with 50+ employees and $50,000+ in contracts under Executive Order 11246 (1965, amended), enforced by OFCCP.
- Contractors must conduct annual utilization analysis (compare workforce demographics to availability in the relevant labor market) and set goals where women or minorities are underutilized.
- Goals must be reasonable and based on availability, not population percentages.
- Failure to meet goals is NOT a violation IF the contractor made good-faith efforts.
- Contractors must document recruitment outreach, selection process fairness, and training efforts.
- Penalties for non-compliance: debarment from federal contracts, back pay orders, conciliation agreements.

3. VOLUNTARY AFFIRMATIVE ACTION - LEGAL under Johnson v. Transportation Agency (1987) conditions.
Definition: An employer voluntarily adopts a program to increase representation of underrepresented groups in its workforce, without a court order and without federal contractor obligation.

Legal conditions (from United Steelworkers v. Weber, 1979, and Johnson v. Santa Clara County, 1987):
(a) The plan must be designed to remedy a 'manifest imbalance' in traditionally segregated job categories (evidenced by comparing workforce to qualified labor pool).
(b) The plan must not 'unnecessarily trammel' the interests of non-beneficiaries - cannot require termination of incumbents, cannot create an absolute bar to non-beneficiary advancement.
(c) The plan must be temporary - ending once the imbalance is remedied.
(d) The plan must be flexible - race/sex can be ONE factor among many, not the sole determinant.

LEGAL DEI practices that do not raise quota concerns:
- Broad recruiting: expanding candidate sourcing to historically underrepresented universities, professional associations, community organizations.
- Mentorship and sponsorship programs open to all but designed with underrepresented groups in mind.
- Employee resource groups (ERGs) that support employees but do not determine selection.
- Inclusive job descriptions, debiased screening (blind review), structured interviews.
- Transparent pay analysis and equal pay audits.
- Inclusion-focused leadership training.
- Supplier diversity programs (pipeline development).

ILLEGAL DEI practices:
- Fixed diversity quotas in hiring or promotions.
- Reserving specific positions for protected groups.
- Selection decisions using race/sex as the sole or determinative factor.
- 'Diversity hires' treated as second-class employees.
- Retaliation against employees who voice opposition to DEI programs.

Post-2023 considerations: Following SFFA v. Harvard, there is increased litigation risk around corporate DEI programs. Edward Blum's America First Legal Foundation and others are filing complaints against corporate affirmative action. Best practice is to (a) document legitimate business rationale for DEI programs, (b) ensure programs are truly inclusive (not exclusionary), (c) tie to measurable business outcomes, (d) ground decisions in business competencies rather than protected class.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between quotas, goals/timetables, and voluntary AA
Specific legal citations: Johnson v. Transportation Agency, EO 11246, Weber, SFFA
Practical guidance on legal DEI practices and post-SFFA considerations

lab_assignments.description: Weekly exercises including job analysis, structured interview design, performance rating calibration, compensation analysis, and engagement surveys

major_assignments.description: Three projects: strategic HR audit of a real company, selection system design, and compensation strategy analysis

midterm_examination.description: Written and applied exam covering strategic HRM, employment law, job analysis, recruitment, selection, training, and performance management (weeks 1-7)

final_project.description: Comprehensive final project integrating HR strategy, analytics, and program design in a realistic organizational case

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected at all lectures and mandatory for case discussion and guest speaker sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce the participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without documented emergency.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of concepts and case approaches is encouraged. All individual assignments including case analyses, job descriptions, interview guides, and written deliverables must be your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying case analyses or HR program designs from consulting reports, the internet, or other students constitutes academic misconduct and will result in a grade of zero and reporting to the academic integrity office.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Excel for analytics exercises, access to O*NET and salary survey databases, and the course textbook. Lab computers are available for analytics software.
